Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Hijack Execution Flow through Path Interception by Unquoted Path
Analysis Description: Unquoted service or shortcut paths that contain spaces and allow path interception by higher-level executables. Defender observes registry service configurations with unquoted paths, file creation of executables in parent directories of unquoted paths, and subsequent process execution from unexpected locations.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of critical services to check for unquoted paths in ImagePath registry keys.
Executables with names matching potential interception targets (e.g., program.exe, net.exe).
Correlation interval between file creation in parent directories and execution of unquoted path process.
Known good service paths for comparison against modified or unquoted values.

Detection Name: User Execution – Malicious Link (click → suspicious egress → download/write → follow-on activity)
Analysis Description: Behavioral chain: (1) a user-facing app (browser/Office/email client) launches a URL or handles a link, then (2) the same process lineage makes an outbound connection to an untrusted domain/IP, (3) a file is downloaded or unpacked to a user-writable location shortly after the click. Optional enrichment: subsequent child execution by LOLBINs.

Log Sources.Name: NSM:Flow
Log Sources.Channel: Suspicious URL patterns, uncommon TLDs, short-lived domains, URL shorteners; HTTP method GET/POST

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlation window (e.g., 15m) between link click / first egress / file write.
Processes considered link sources: chrome.exe, msedge.exe, firefox.exe, winword.exe, outlook.exe, teams.exe.
User-writable directories to monitor (%USERPROFILE%\Downloads, %TEMP%, %APPDATA%\*, OneDrive caches).
High-risk TLD and domain list (e.g., .top .xyz .monster; newly observed domains/NOD).
Corporate CDNs/update hosts to reduce false positives.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Ignore Process Interrupts
Analysis Description: PowerShell or script execution with parameters that suppress errors or ignore user interrupts, such as -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue. Defender perspective: detecting discrepancies between suppressed error arguments and continued execution behavior.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of PowerShell cmdlets where suppressed error handling is suspicious (e.g., Invoke-Expression, Invoke-WebRequest).
Frequency of suppressed error actions within time window that should trigger detection.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for T1505.004 - Malicious IIS Components
Analysis Description: Adversary installs or modifies IIS components (ISAPI filters, extensions, or modules) using DLL files registered via configuration changes or administrative tools like AppCmd.exe. These components intercept or manipulate HTTP requests/responses for persistence or C2.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:System
Log Sources.Channel: Changes to applicationhost.config or DLLs loaded by w3wp.exe

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Microsoft-IIS-Configuration
Log Sources.Channel: Module or ISAPI filter registration events

Mutable Elements.Description: Adjustable time frame for detecting chained events (e.g., config change + module load)
Scope detection to specific users or roles allowed to modify IIS components
Specific directories such as %windir%\System32\inetsrv\ for DLL monitoring
Entropy or name patterns to flag suspicious DLLs registered as components
Restrict to DLLs loaded by w3wp.exe or invoked via AppCmd.exe

Detection Name: Detect unauthorized or suspicious Hardware Additions (USB/Thunderbolt/Network)
Analysis Description: Chain: (1) a new external device is recognized by Windows (USB/Thunderbolt/PCIe) or a new block device appears; (2) within a short window, the same user/session spawns processes or the OS mounts a new volume; (3) optional follow-on activity such as HID keystroke injection, DMA driver load, or new network interface MAC on DHCP. Correlate Security EID 6416 / Kernel-PnP with sysmon and DHCP/network metadata.

Mutable Elements.Description: Vendor/Product IDs that are approved (e.g., keyboards, mice). Unknown/rare VID:PID raise risk.
Allow-listed bus types for server classes (e.g., USB disabled on DCs).
Correlation window between device recognition and follow-on process/mount/network activity (e.g., 10m–60m).
Known NIC/USB-NIC MAC addresses allowed by policy.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Phishing across platforms.
Analysis Description: Unusual inbound email activity where attachments or embedded URLs are delivered to users followed by execution of new processes or suspicious document behavior. Detection involves correlating email metadata, file creation, and network activity after a phishing message is received.

Log Sources.Name: m365:unified
Log Sources.Channel: Send/Receive: Emails with suspicious sender domains, spoofed headers, or anomalous attachment types

Mutable Elements.Description: Attachment types considered high risk (e.g., .exe, .js, .vbs, .scr, macro-enabled docs).
Whitelist of known trusted senders to reduce false positives.

Detection Name: Detection of Remote Data Staging Prior to Exfiltration
Analysis Description: Detects file transfers or mounting operations from remote hosts followed by write actions into a local staging directory, often using SMB or remote shell activity.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:PowerShell
Log Sources.Channel: CommandLine=copy-item or robocopy from UNC path

Mutable Elements.Description: Common directories such as C:\Temp, Downloads, or hidden folders used for remote staging
UNC paths like \\10.* or \\domain\share indicating lateral data staging
Usage of robocopy, xcopy, copy-item, or scheduled tasks performing cross-host copies

Detection Name: Detect Logon Script Modifications and Execution
Analysis Description: Detects adversary use of logon script configuration via Group Policy or user object attributes, followed by script execution post-authentication. Behavior includes modification of script path or file, then process execution under user logon context.

Mutable Elements.Description: Defenders may tune for known script locations such as NETLOGON, SYSVOL, or \domain\sysvol\*.bat/.ps1
May be scoped to user logon hours or first X minutes post-authentication
Organizations may focus on specific users/groups with high privilege or remote access

Detection Name: Internal Proxy Behavior via Lateral Host-to-Host C2 Relay
Analysis Description: Anomalous process (e.g., rundll32, svchost, cmd) initiates connections to internal peer hosts not seen in typical communication baselines, used to proxy or forward traffic internally, often using SMB, RPC, or high ports.

Mutable Elements.Description: Tune based on known host-to-host communications that are rare (e.g., workstation-to-workstation).
Focus on unusual internal traffic on ports like 1080, 8080, 4444, or SMB over non-standard ports.
Correlate unusual traffic bursts with new process execution.

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Visual Basic Execution (VBS/VBA/VBScript)
Analysis Description: Detects execution of VB-based scripts or macros (VBS/VBA/VBScript) through cscript.exe/wscript.exe, Office-based process chains, or HTA usage. Focuses on chained behavior: Office or HTML container spawns script host > script host spawns PowerShell, network connections, or process injection.

Mutable Elements.Description: Microsoft Word/Excel or mshta.exe spawning wscript.exe/cscript.exe.
Script execution by non-admin users or service accounts.
Script execution outside normal business hours or patching cycle.
High entropy indicative of obfuscation or encoding in the script.
Loading of vbscript.dll, scrrun.dll, or other scripting engine modules.

Detection Name: Detection of Exfiltration Over Alternate Network Interfaces
Analysis Description: Execution of file transfer or network access activity through non-primary interfaces (e.g., WiFi, Bluetooth, cellular) by processes not typically associated with such behavior (e.g., rundll32, powershell, regsvr32).

Mutable Elements.Description: Filter for specific interface categories (e.g., WiFi, Bluetooth, 4G).
Tunable for environment-specific large file access events pre-transfer.
Temporal correlation window for file read followed by network activity.

Detection Name: Detection of Remote Service Session Hijacking
Analysis Description: Detection of anomalous RDP or remote service session activity where a logon session is hijacked rather than newly created. Indicators include mismatched user credentials vs. active session tokens, service session takeovers without corresponding successful logon events, or RDP shadowing activity without user consent.

Mutable Elements.Description: Mapping of users to hosts they are expected to access; deviations indicate possible hijacking.
Threshold for detecting rapid pivoting via hijacked sessions.

Detection Name: Exploit Public-Facing Application – multi-signal correlation (request → error → post-exploit process/egress)
Analysis Description: Adversary sends crafted HTTP/S (or other service) input to an Internet-facing app (IIS/ASP.NET, API, device portal). Chain: (1) abnormal request patterns to public endpoint → (2) elevated 4xx/5xx or unusual methods/paths → (3) server process (w3wp.exe/other service) spawns shell/LOLbins or loads non-standard modules → (4) optional outbound callback from the host/container.

Log Sources.Name: ApplicationLog:IIS
Log Sources.Channel: IIS W3C logs in C:\inetpub\logs\LogFiles\W3SVC* (spikes in 5xx, RCE/SQLi/path traversal/JNDI patterns)

Mutable Elements.Description: List of public IPs/hostnames that front apps; used to scope web log and Zeek/proxy data.
Regex set for exploit-like inputs (../, union select, cmd=, ${jndi:, rO0AB (Java serialization), %00, ${env:}, ${${::-j}ndi}).
Spike threshold for HTTP status 5xx/4xx per client or URI (e.g., >5 in 5m).
Correlation horizon between request, error, process spawn, and egress (e.g., 15 minutes).
Known child processes of app pools (e.g., msbuild.exe in CI) to reduce false positives.

Detection Name: Detection of Proxy Execution via Trusted Signed Binaries Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Execution of trusted, Microsoft-signed binaries such as rundll32.exe, msiexec.exe, or regsvr32.exe used to execute externally hosted, unsigned, or suspicious payloads through command-line parameters or network retrieval.

Mutable Elements.Description: Used to profile unexpected parent-child relationships (e.g., regsvr32.exe not launched by explorer.exe)
List of known signed binaries allowed for execution (e.g., msiexec.exe, regsvr32.exe)
Regex to match suspicious arguments, such as URLs, script paths, or DLL entrypoints
Filter to suppress activity contacting legitimate enterprise domains

Detection Name: Internal Website and System Content Defacement via UI or Messaging Modifications
Analysis Description: Adversary modifies internal UI messages (e.g., login banners, desktop wallpapers) or hosted intranet web pages by creating or altering content files using scripts or unauthorized access. Often preceded by privilege escalation or web shell deployment.

Mutable Elements.Description: Location of web content or system UI config files that may vary across deployments (e.g., %SystemRoot%\Web, %APPDATA%\wallpaper.jpg)
Allowed hours for file/content modification events; defacement likely occurs during off-hours
System or domain accounts used to perform the modifications may be anomalous

Detection Name: Credential Dumping from SAM via Registry Dump and Local File Access
Analysis Description: An adversary running with SYSTEM-level privileges executes commands or accesses registry keys to dump the SAM hive or directly reads sensitive local files from the config directory. This behavior often involves sequential access to HKLM\SAM, HKLM\SYSTEM, and creation of .save or .dmp files, enabling offline hash extraction.

Mutable Elements.Description: Detectable variations include `reg save`, `reg.exe save`, or PowerShell equivalents for dumping SAM/SYSTEM hives.
Defenders can tune based on dump file path patterns (e.g., `%TEMP%\sam.save`, `C:\Users\Public\*.dmp`).
Tune for HKLM\SAM, HKLM\SYSTEM or access via direct \Device\Harddisk paths.
Temporal gap between SAM and SYSTEM hive dumping can be tuned (e.g., 3 minutes).
Useful for suppressing known-good access (e.g., backup tools).

Detection Name: Detect WMI Event Subscription for Persistence via WmiPrvSE Process and MOF Compilation
Analysis Description: Monitor for creation of WMI EventFilter, EventConsumer, and FilterToConsumerBinding objects through WMI or MOF file execution. Detect command-line execution of mofcomp.exe, usage of Register-WmiEvent via PowerShell, and anomalous child processes of WmiPrvSE.exe that indicate triggered execution. Look for lateral anomalies in process lineage and WMI logging channels.

Mutable Elements.Description: Defines temporal correlation range between WMI creation and child process execution
Tune for specific accounts (e.g., SYSTEM or attacker-controlled users)
Used to exclude known benign consumers triggered via WMI (e.g., backup tools)
Defines what constitutes anomalous spawning from WmiPrvSE.exe

Detection Name: Encrypted or Encoded File Payload Detection Strategy
Analysis Description: Detection of processes that load or decode encrypted/encoded files in memory and subsequently execute or inject them, indicating payload unpacking or memory-resident malware.

Mutable Elements.Description: Path of decoder utilities (e.g., certutil.exe, powershell.exe) can vary across environments.
Base64/hex strings used may change per encoded payload.
The duration between file decode and execution may differ across implementations.

Detection Name: Backup Software Discovery via CLI, Registry, and Process Inspection (T1518.002)
Analysis Description: Defender observes execution of commands like tasklist, sc query, reg query, or PowerShell WMI/Registry queries targeting known backup products (e.g., Veeam, Acronis, CrashPlan). Behavior often includes parent-child lineage involving PowerShell or cmd.exe with discovery syntax, and enumeration of services, directories, or registry paths tied to backup software.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of software vendors to match in command-line or registry queries
Focus on low-privilege or interactive user contexts rather than service accounts
Flag execution from scripting tools, interpreters, or LOLBins

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Keylogging Activity Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Monitors suspicious usage of Windows API calls like SetWindowsHookEx, GetKeyState, or polling functions within non-UI service processes, combined with Registry or driver modifications.

Mutable Elements.Description: Scope to sensitive GUI/session processes like winlogon.exe or osk.exe
Can be tuned to 0x1fffff for full-access injection detection
Tunable for sustained polling or multiple registry edits in short succession

Detection Name: Cross-host C2 via Removable Media Relay
Analysis Description: Behavioral sequence where removable media is mounted, files are written/updated, and subsequently read/executed on a separate host, suggesting removable-media relay communication.

Mutable Elements.Description: Adjust drive letters used in detection (e.g., E:, F:, G:) depending on enterprise usage.
Tunable window for file write on one host followed by file read or execution on another (e.g., within 10 minutes).
Common naming schemes for payload, tasking, or exfil files (e.g., task.txt, beacon.log, data.bin).

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Dynamic API Resolution via Hash-Based Function Lookups
Analysis Description: Behavioral chain involving suspicious use of GetProcAddress and LoadLibrary following memory allocation and manual mapping, often paired with low entropy strings, abnormal API use without static import tables, or delayed module load behaviors.

Log Sources.Name: etw:Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Process
Log Sources.Channel: API tracing / stack tracing via ETW or telemetry-based EDR

Mutable Elements.Description: Tunable logic to flag suspicious modules used without static IAT entries
Correlates module load to suspicious memory allocation or API lookup within timeframe
Used to detect obfuscated strings or hashed function names
Optional filtering of known safe modules or patterns from telemetry

Detection Name: Detection of Malicious or Unauthorized Software Extensions
Analysis Description: Installation or execution of a malicious browser or IDE extension, followed by abnormal registry entries or outbound network connections from the host application

Mutable Elements.Description: Path of browser or IDE launching subprocesses—may vary depending on installed applications
Legitimate parent-child process relationships for known safe extensions
Expected registry keys under HKCU/HKLM for installed extensions
Tunable interval to correlate extension install with follow-on C2 traffic

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of User Discovery via Local and Remote Enumeration
Analysis Description: Adversary launches built-in system tools (e.g., whoami, query user, net user) or scripts that enumerate user account information via local execution or remote API queries (e.g., WMI, PowerShell).

Mutable Elements.Description: Identify if enumeration originates from non-interactive shell or system service
Tune temporal grouping of enumeration + lateral movement attempts
Flag unexpected users issuing enumeration commands (e.g., service accounts)

Detection Name: Cross-Platform Behavioral Detection of Scheduled Task/Job Abuse
Analysis Description: Detects creation or modification of scheduled tasks using schtasks.exe, at.exe, or COM objects followed by execution of outlier processes tied to the scheduled job.

Mutable Elements.Description: Unexpected user or account context initiating the task.
Suspicious binaries or script usage tied to scheduled tasks.
Lookback window to correlate process execution after task registration.

Detection Name: Detect Persistence via Malicious Outlook Rules
Analysis Description: Adversary uses a tool like Ruler or MFCMapi to create a malicious Outlook rule that triggers execution upon receipt of a crafted email. On email delivery, Outlook executes the rule, resulting in code execution (e.g., launching mshta.exe or PowerShell). Outlook spawns a non-standard child process, often unsanctioned, without user interaction.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Application
Log Sources.Channel: Outlook rule execution failure or abnormal rule execution context

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:PowerShell
Log Sources.Channel: PowerShell launched from outlook.exe or triggered without user invocation

Mutable Elements.Description: Outlook may spawn mshta.exe, powershell.exe, or wscript.exe depending on attacker payload
Rule execution may depend on message subject, sender, or message header content
Legitimate Outlook activity should not spawn scripting or interpreter processes
Execution may occur with delay after message receipt or folder interaction

Detection Name: Account Manipulation Behavior Chain Detection
Analysis Description: Account attribute changes (e.g., password set, group membership, servicePrincipalName, logon hours) correlated with unusual process lineage or timing, indicating privilege escalation or persistence via valid accounts.

Mutable Elements.Description: Time between suspicious process and account change (e.g., 5m).
Customize group list (e.g., Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins) to monitor.
Flag if account modifier != modified user (potential hijack).

Detection Name: Detection of Application Window Enumeration via API or Scripting
Analysis Description: Processes using Win32 API calls (e.g., EnumWindows, GetForegroundWindow) or scripting tools (e.g., PowerShell, VBScript) to enumerate open windows. These often appear with reconnaissance or data collection TTPs.

Mutable Elements.Description: Tune to focus on suspicious function calls (e.g., user32.dll!EnumWindows).
Detect behavior from non-interactive or low-privileged users where enumeration is uncommon.
Shorten detection scope to rapid successive window enumeration attempts.

Detection Name: Detect abuse of Windows BITS Jobs for download, execution and persistence
Analysis Description: Behavioral chain: (1) An actor creates or modifies a BITS job via bitsadmin.exe, PowerShell BITS cmdlets, or COM; (2) the job performs HTTP(S)/SMB network transfers while the owning user is logged on; (3) upon job completion/error, BITS launches a notify command (SetNotifyCmdLine) from svchost.exe -k netsvcs -s BITS, often establishing persistence by keeping long-lived jobs. The strategy correlates process creation, command/script telemetry, BITS-Client operational events, and network connections initiated by BITS.

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlation window linking job creation, transfer, and notify execution (e.g., 30m–24h depending on environment and BITS retry behavior).
Allow-list of corporate update/CDN endpoints that legitimately use BITS (WSUS, MEMCM, vendor updaters).
BITSAdmin flags of interest (/transfer, /addfile, /SetNotifyCmdLine, /resume, /setcustomheaders, /setminretrydelay).
Known risky binaries or folders (e.g., %TEMP%\*.exe, powershell.exe, cmd.exe) used as BITS notify commands.
Scope by interactive users, service accounts, or high-value targets (admins/servers) to reduce benign noise.
Definition of external/non-corp destinations for network correlation.
Maximum age or retry count for benign jobs before flagging persistence (e.g., >3 days or retry>20).

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for T1542.001 Pre-OS Boot: System Firmware
Analysis Description: Unexpected write operations to BIOS/UEFI firmware regions or EFI boot partitions that do not correlate with legitimate vendor firmware updates. API calls or utilities such as fwupdate.exe or vendor flash tools executed from non-administrative or non-IT management accounts. Suspicious raw disk writes targeting System Firmware GUID partitions followed by abnormal reboot sequences.

Mutable Elements.Description: Legitimate vendor tools permitted to perform firmware flashing or BIOS updates.
Expected time periods for approved firmware updates, used for correlating suspicious activity outside patch cycles.
Baseline hashes of vendor BIOS/UEFI firmware for integrity comparison.

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Asynchronous Procedure Call (APC) Injection via Remote Thread Queuing
Analysis Description: Detects malicious injection behavior involving memory allocation, remote thread queuing via APC (e.g., QueueUserAPC), and altered thread context within another live process to execute unauthorized code under legitimate context.

Mutable Elements.Description: Processes that are rarely or never valid targets for legitimate APC queuing (e.g., lsass.exe, winlogon.exe)
The number of APCs queued within a short time window that could signal abuse
Expected latency between memory allocation and thread execution through APC
Used to filter based on expected vs unexpected user to target process pairings

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Lua Scripting Abuse
Analysis Description: Detects execution of Lua interpreters or scripts (.lua), especially when correlated with suspicious parent processes or file drop events, indicating malicious use of embedded scripting.

Mutable Elements.Description: May vary depending on delivery vector (e.g., explorer.exe, cmd.exe, rundll32.exe)
Used to correlate file drop and execution of Lua scripts in close succession.

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Input Capture Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Monitors for abnormal process behavior and API calls like SetWindowsHookEx, GetAsyncKeyState, or device input polling commonly used for keystroke logging.

Mutable Elements.Description: Can be scoped to sensitive GUI processes like explorer.exe or winlogon.exe
Time threshold for detecting multiple suspicious accesses

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Network Share Connection Removal via CLI and SMB Disconnects
Analysis Description: Detects network share disconnection attempts using command-line tools like net use /delete, PowerShell Remove-SmbMapping, and correlation with process lineage and SMB session teardown activity.

Mutable Elements.Description: Adjustable window to correlate CLI disconnection command with SMB session teardown (e.g., 5 mins)
Used to filter on non-interactive users or highly privileged accounts
Patterns to match `net use \\host\share /delete`, `Remove-SmbMapping`, or suspicious batched disconnections
Tunable list of shares likely targeted (e.g., ADMIN$, C$, IPC$)

Detection Name: Detect Modification of Authentication Processes Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Detects modification of LSASS and authentication DLLs, suspicious registry changes to password filter packages, and abnormal process access to lsass.exe. Correlates registry modifications, DLL loads, and process handle access events.

Mutable Elements.Description: Specific LSASS and password filter registry paths monitored for modification.
Correlation window between registry change, DLL load, and lsass.exe access.

Detection Name: Post-Credential Dump Password Cracking Detection via Suspicious File Access and Hash Analysis Tools
Analysis Description: Use of hash-cracking tools (e.g., John the Ripper, Hashcat) after credential dumping, combined with high CPU usage or GPU invocation via unsigned binaries accessing password hash files

Mutable Elements.Description: Match execution against known cracking toolnames like hashcat.exe, john.exe, etc.
Watch for access to common hash dump locations (e.g., SAM, SYSTEM, NTDS.dit)
Run context: local interactive user vs. scheduled task or remote session

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of PE Injection via Remote Memory Mapping
Analysis Description: Detects PE injection through a behavioral sequence where one process opens (OpenProcess) a handle to another, allocates remote memory (VirtualAllocEx), writes a PE header (MZ) or shellcode (WriteProcessMemory), then initiates a new thread (CreateRemoteThread or NtCreateThreadEx) in that process—executing injected code in memory without touching disk. Optional: injects a trampoline or shellcode that unpacks/reflectively maps the payload.

Mutable Elements.Description: Controls for detecting high-entropy memory writes indicating shellcode or encrypted PE
High-value or sensitive processes that should never have remote threads injected
Max allowed delay between memory write and thread execution
Used to filter legitimate process hierarchies vs anomalous injection sources

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Spearphishing Links
Analysis Description: Correlation of inbound emails with embedded links followed by user-driven browser navigation to suspicious or obfuscated domains. Detection chain includes malicious URL in email → user click recorded in Office logs → browser process spawning unusual child processes (e.g., PowerShell, cmd) or download activity.

Log Sources.Name: m365:unified
Log Sources.Channel: Send/Receive: Inbound emails containing embedded or shortened URLs

Mutable Elements.Description: List of monitored top-level domains commonly abused in phishing (e.g., .xyz, .top, .tk).
Domains like bit.ly, tinyurl.com flagged for deeper expansion/inspection.
Time threshold between URL click and suspicious process execution.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Data Encoding in C2 Channels
Analysis Description: Atypical processes (e.g., powershell.exe, regsvr32.exe) encode large outbound traffic using Base64 or other character encodings; this traffic is sent over uncommon ports or embedded in protocol fields (e.g., HTTP cookies or headers).

Log Sources.Name: NSM:Flow
Log Sources.Channel: Unusual Base64-encoded content in URI, headers, or POST body

Mutable Elements.Description: Adjust to accommodate legitimate compression or encryption patterns in normal web traffic
Define expected processes initiating outbound traffic to reduce false positives
Set threshold for how far traffic deviates from baseline protocol structure or size

Detection Name: Boot or Logon Initialization Scripts Detection Strategy
Analysis Description: Monitoring modification and execution of user or system logon scripts such as in registry Run keys or startup folders.

Mutable Elements.Description: Registry path that may vary by user or policy configuration.
Can be tuned to known parent processes to reduce false positives.
Logon activity clustered during specific user shifts.

Detection Name: Detect AS-REP Roasting Attempts (T1558.004)
Analysis Description: Detects AS-REP roasting attempts by monitoring for Kerberos AS-REQ/AS-REP authentication patterns where preauthentication is disabled (Event ID 4768 with Pre-Auth Type 0). Correlates these requests with subsequent service ticket activity (Event ID 4769) and anomalies such as requests using weak RC4 encryption (etype 0x17). Excessive enumeration of accounts with 'Do not require Kerberos preauthentication' set in Active Directory is another key detection point.

Mutable Elements.Description: Baseline of accounts legitimately configured without Kerberos preauthentication; deviations may indicate adversary enumeration.
Number of AS-REQ/AS-REP exchanges per account within a short timeframe; higher counts may indicate AS-REP roasting.
Permitted Kerberos encryption algorithms; RC4 usage (etype 0x17) should be closely monitored.
Correlation window for linking AS-REQs, AS-REPs, and subsequent service ticket requests.

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Local Group Enumeration Across OS Platforms
Analysis Description: Detects attempts to enumerate local groups via Net.exe, PowerShell, or native API calls that precede lateral movement or privilege abuse.

Mutable Elements.Description: Time window between group enumeration and lateral movement or privilege escalation activity.
Whether the process was executed by a privileged or low-privilege account.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Spearphishing via a Service across OS Platforms
Analysis Description: Inbound spearphishing attempts delivered via third-party services (e.g., Gmail, LinkedIn messages) leading to malicious file downloads or browser-initiated script execution. Defender view includes correlation of external service logins, unexpected file write operations, and suspicious descendant processes spawned from productivity or browser applications.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of third-party services (e.g., Gmail, LinkedIn, Dropbox) relevant to the organization’s threat profile.
Process lineage and parent-child execution relationships considered abnormal (e.g., outlook.exe → powershell.exe).
Correlates file creation and outbound connection activity within a tunable time period after message receipt.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Safe Mode Boot Abuse
Analysis Description: Abuse of safe mode via BCD modification, boot configuration utilities (bcdedit.exe, bootcfg.exe), and registry persistence under SafeBoot keys. Defender view: suspicious boot configuration changes correlated with registry edits that enable adversary persistence or disable defenses.

Mutable Elements.Description: Customize monitored registry paths for safe mode service additions.
Whitelist legitimate administrative use of bcdedit/bootcfg for troubleshooting.
Correlate registry modifications and boot configuration commands within a short timeframe.

Detection Name: Detection of Masqueraded Tasks or Services with Suspicious Naming and Execution
Analysis Description: Creation or modification of Windows services or scheduled tasks with names or descriptions mimicking legitimate entries, followed by anomalous execution of untrusted binaries or LOLBAS.

Mutable Elements.Description: Similarity threshold for comparing new task/service names to known legitimate names (e.g., Levenshtein distance)
Confidence level required for allowing a binary, often from unsigned or untrusted source
Whether the execution came from SYSTEM, service accounts, or user contexts

Detection Name: Exploitation of Remote Services – multi-platform lateral movement detection
Analysis Description: Correlates inbound network access to remote service ports (e.g., SMB/RPC 445/135, RDP 3389, WinRM 5985/5986) with near-time instability in the target service (crash, abnormal restart), suspicious child process creation under the service, and post-access lateral-movement behaviors. The chain indicates likely exploitation rather than normal administration.

Log Sources.Name: NSM:Flow
Log Sources.Channel: Inbound connections to 445, 3389, 5985-5986 with high error/connection-reset rate, followed by new outbound sessions from the same host to internal assets within short interval.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of monitored service ports (default: 445,135,3389,5985,5986,1433,3306).
Correlation window between inbound access and crash/child-process (default: 10 minutes).
Known management networks to suppress benign admin traffic.
Percent of failed/aborted connections to treat as anomalous (default: 30%).

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Steganographic Abuse in File & Script Execution
Analysis Description: Detects execution of image viewers or PowerShell scripts accessing or decoding files with mismatched MIME headers or embedded script-like byte patterns; often correlated with suspicious parent-child process lineage and outbound connections.

Mutable Elements.Description: Tune to identify image editors/viewers invoking script interpreters (e.g., `mspaint.exe` > `powershell.exe`)
Adjust tolerance for image file headers that do not match file extensions or content structure
Define the temporal range to correlate decoding → execution → network beaconing

Detection Name: Account Access Removal via Multi-Platform Audit Correlation
Analysis Description: Correlated user account modification (reset, disable, deletion) events with anomalous process lineage (e.g., PowerShell or net.exe from an interactive session), especially outside of IT admin change windows or by non-admin users.

Mutable Elements.Description: Account performing the operation (e.g., Domain Admins vs. local users)
Alert only on actions outside of maintenance windows
Detect suspicious process lineage (e.g., powershell.exe launching net.exe)

Detection Name: Detect Abuse of Windows Time Providers for Persistence
Analysis Description: Behavioral correlation of privileged registry key creation under the W32Time TimeProviders path combined with a new DLL written to disk and potential process activity by LocalService. Indicates abuse of Time Providers for persistence.

Mutable Elements.Description: May need to be tuned to only monitor `W32Time\TimeProviders` subkey path for performance optimization
Should focus on activity from administrative or SYSTEM accounts
Controls correlation window between registry modification and DLL drop
Used for anomaly scoring on DLL path patterns (e.g., random names or temp directories)

Detection Name: Detection of Data Exfiltration via Removable Media
Analysis Description: Detects removable drive insertion followed by unusual file access, compression, or staging activity by unauthorized users or unexpected processes.

Mutable Elements.Description: Filter on removable (e.g., USB) drives only.
Exclude known, approved backup or sync utilities.
Limit correlation of file access and device mount to a defined window (e.g., <5 minutes).

Detection Name: Behavior-chain detection for T1132.001 Data Encoding: Standard Encoding (Base64/Hex/MIME) across Windows, Linux, macOS, ESXi
Analysis Description: Process invokes a standard encoder (e.g., PowerShell -enc, certutil -encode, base64 via .NET/Invoke-Expression) or emits long Base64/hex literals → shortly followed by outbound network egress with high bytes_out:bytes_in ratio or HTTP headers/payloads containing Base64/MIME blocks.

Mutable Elements.Description: Shannon entropy cutoff to consider payload suspicious (e.g., > 4.5–5.0 for HTTP body).
Min continuous Base64 token length in command lines/script blocks to alert (e.g., > 100 chars).
Correlation window between encoding event and egress (default 10m).
Legitimate tools (e.g., backup agents) that routinely encode/compress data.
Minimum ratio to treat flow as asymmetric (e.g., ≥ 4:1).

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Masquerading Across Platforms via Metadata and Execution Discrepancy
Analysis Description: Adversary renames LOLBINs or deploys binaries with spoofed file names, internal PE metadata, or misleading icons to appear legitimate. File creation is followed by execution or service registration inconsistent with known usage.

Mutable Elements.Description: Compare executable file name with PE metadata OriginalFilename field
Tune based on expected installation directories for signed binaries
Correlation window between file creation and service/process execution

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Hidden Windows
Analysis Description: Suspicious use of scripting parameters or registry edits to hide process windows (e.g., powershell.exe -WindowStyle Hidden, or registry modifications pushing window positions off screen). Defender view: correlation of hidden execution with anomalous process lineage or hVNC-like CreateDesktop API calls.

Mutable Elements.Description: Restrict to processes where hidden execution is unexpected (e.g., PowerShell, cmd, wscript).
Correlate hidden execution with suspicious parent processes to reduce false positives.

Detection Name: Domain Account Enumeration Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Adversary enumeration of domain accounts using net.exe, PowerShell, WMI, or LDAP queries from non-domain controllers or non-admin endpoints.

Mutable Elements.Description: Detect variations of 'net user /domain', 'Get-ADUser', 'Get-ADGroupMember'.
Tune detection for bursts of enumeration commands or search queries.
Restrict detection to non-DC or non-admin systems where such commands are unexpected.

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection Strategy for Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol
Analysis Description: Detects unusual outbound file transfer behavior using protocols like FTP, SMB, SMTP, or DNS, involving non-standard processes, off-hour activity, or uncommonly high volume.

Mutable Elements.Description: Set threshold for outbound volume (e.g., >50MB in a single connection).
Allow-listed protocols in use for specific machines or users (e.g., FTP allowed for backups).
Define allowed time-of-day windows (e.g., flag after-hours file transfer).
Identify anomalous parent-child process relationships (e.g., `winword.exe` spawning `ftp.exe`).

Detection Name: Detection of Mutex-Based Execution Guardrails Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Adversary-created named mutex using system APIs (e.g., CreateMutexW) followed by conditional process termination or alternate code path indicating malware avoiding reinfection.

Mutable Elements.Description: Filter out common benign mutex names; highlight suspicious high-entropy/dynamic names.
Limit alerting to non-standard parent-child relationships indicative of malware staging or self-spawning.
Correlate mutex creation + rapid process exit or lack of further activity within a short timeframe.

Detection Name: IDE Tunneling Detection via Process, File, and Network Behaviors
Analysis Description: Detection of the creation of VSCode or JetBrains CLI tunneling profiles followed by persistent remote access via IDE-integrated tunnels, potentially authenticated via GitHub or JetBrains accounts.

Mutable Elements.Description: Used to define the temporal proximity between tunnel profile creation and outbound connection.
Domain patterns for tunnel endpoints may change with IDE versions or organizations.
Helps filter tunnel usage from trusted developer accounts.

Detection Name: Detect Suspicious Access to Windows Credential Manager
Analysis Description: Detects unauthorized access to Windows Credential Manager through anomalous process execution (vaultcmd.exe, rundll32.exe keymgr.dll), suspicious API calls (CredEnumerateA), or direct file access to Credential Locker files. Correlates process creation with subsequent file reads of .vcrd/.vpol files under user Credential Locker directories.

Mutable Elements.Description: Credential Locker paths such as %Systemdrive%\Users\*\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Credentials and %Systemdrive%\Users\*\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Vault
Correlation window between process execution, file access, and API calls
Baseline of expected administrative/service accounts with legitimate Credential Manager access

Detection Name: Detection of Mail Protocol-Based C2 Activity (SMTP, IMAP, POP3)
Analysis Description: Detects unauthorized use of SMTP/IMAP/POP3 by suspicious binaries (e.g., PowerShell, rundll32) to exfiltrate data or beacon via email, often bypassing proxy or content filters.

Mutable Elements.Description: Limit to uncommon clients (e.g., scripts or CLI tools using .NET SMTP libraries)
Typically 25, 587, 993, 995, or 465 – flag anomalies
Flag suspicious attachments (e.g., .zip, .7z, .bin)

Detection Name: Behavior-chain detection for T1134.005 Access Token Manipulation: SID-History Injection (Windows)
Analysis Description: Detection of unauthorized modification of Active Directory SID-History attributes to escalate privileges. This chain involves: (1) privileged operations or API calls to DsAddSidHistory or related AD modification functions, (2) observed attribute changes in SID-History (Event ID 5136), (3) new logon sessions where the token includes unexpected or privileged SID-History values, and (4) follow-on resource access using elevated privileges derived from SID-History injection.

Log Sources.Name: etw:Microsoft-Windows-Directory-Services-SAM
Log Sources.Channel: api_call: Calls to DsAddSidHistory or related RPC operations

Mutable Elements.Description: Approved migration windows or known SID-History population events.
Correlation window between attribute change and suspicious logon activity (default 15–30 minutes).
List of sensitive SIDs (e.g., Enterprise Admins, Domain Admins) that should never appear in SID-History.
Exclude trusted migration service accounts or pre-approved administrative tasks.
Raise alerts when a token contains more than X SID-History entries (default X=2).

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Disk Wipe via Direct Disk Access and Destructive Commands
Analysis Description: Unusual direct disk access attempts (e.g., use of \.\PhysicalDrive notation), abnormal writes to MBR/boot sectors, and installation of kernel drivers that grant raw disk access. Correlate anomalous process creation with disk modification attempts and driver loads.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Sysmon
Log Sources.Channel: Raw disk write access via \\.\PhysicalDrive* or \\.\C:

Mutable Elements.Description: Legitimate disk imaging or backup tools may trigger raw disk access — must be excluded per environment.
Correlate disk access, driver load, and process execution within a short timeframe to minimize false positives.

Detection Name: Detection of Malicious Code Execution via InstallUtil.exe
Analysis Description: Execution of InstallUtil.exe from .NET framework directories with arguments specifying non-standard or attacker-supplied assemblies, especially when followed by suspicious child process creation or script execution. Detection also includes correlation of newly created binaries prior to InstallUtil invocation and anomalous command-line usage compared to historical baselines.

Mutable Elements.Description: Regex pattern for InstallUtil.exe in .NET directories; tune to exclude known good administrative scripts
Patterns for identifying suspicious assemblies (e.g., in temp folders, user profiles)
List of suspicious child processes spawned from InstallUtil.exe (e.g., cmd.exe, powershell.exe, rundll32.exe)
Time correlation window between file creation of assembly and its execution via InstallUtil.exe

Detection Name: Detection of Credential Harvesting via API Hooking
Analysis Description: Detects credential harvesting via userland API hooking (e.g., SetWindowsHookEx, IAT, or inline patching) by correlating memory modifications with hook installation functions and suspicious module loads in credential-sensitive processes like lsass.exe, explorer.exe, or winlogon.exe.

Mutable Elements.Description: Credential-sensitive targets (e.g., explorer.exe, winlogon.exe) may vary by environment
Tuning for access rights like 0x1FFFFF for full access vs. thread injection
Correlate memory access and hook setup in short windows (5–10 seconds)

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Malicious File Deletion
Analysis Description: Detects adversary behavior deleting artifacts (e.g., dropped payloads, evidence files) using native or external utilities (e.g., del, erase, SDelete). Detects deletion events correlated with unusual process lineage or timing post-execution.

Mutable Elements.Description: Defines correlation window after suspicious binary execution or login session.
Focuses on deletion of temp files, malware staging dirs, or known indicators.
Privilege level or impersonated user deleting sensitive files.

Detection Name: Detect Time-Based Evasion via Sleep, Timer Loops, and Delayed Execution
Analysis Description: Process creation involving suspicious delays (e.g., Sleep, ping -n loops, WaitForSingleObject), followed by sensitive system access or lateral movement behaviors.

Mutable Elements.Description: Defines maximum allowable sleep duration in milliseconds before triggering anomaly detection.
Temporal window between initial process and next stage (e.g., lateral movement or persistence), used to correlate dormant activity.
Whether the activity occurs in SYSTEM or user context may affect legitimacy scoring.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Encrypted Channel via Symmetric Cryptography across OS Platforms
Analysis Description: Processes that typically do not perform cryptographic operations loading symmetric encryption libraries (e.g., bcryptprimitives.dll, aes.dll), then initiating outbound connections with high-entropy payloads. Defender correlates process creation, DLL load, and anomalous encrypted traffic patterns.

Mutable Elements.Description: Processes normally expected to use symmetric crypto (e.g., disk encryption, secure messaging).
Minimum payload entropy score for flagging unusual encrypted sessions.
Correlation window between module load and encrypted connection creation.

Detection Name: Detect Forged Kerberos Golden Tickets (T1558.001)
Analysis Description: Detects forged Kerberos Golden Tickets by correlating anomalous Kerberos ticket lifetimes, unexpected encryption types (e.g., RC4 in modern domains), malformed fields in logon/logoff events, and TGS requests without preceding TGT requests. Also monitors for abnormal patterns of access associated with elevated privileges across multiple systems.

Mutable Elements.Description: Kerberos TGT ticket lifetime exceeding default domain duration; tunable to environment-specific policies.
Valid encryption algorithms for Kerberos tickets; anomalies (e.g., RC4) may indicate forgery.
Baseline of privileged accounts expected to perform Kerberos operations; deviations indicate suspicious activity.
Expected processes interacting with lsass.exe; deviations may indicate credential dumping.

Detection Name: Detection of Disabled or Modified System Firewalls across OS Platforms.
Analysis Description: Detection of firewall tampering by monitoring processes executing netsh, PowerShell Set-NetFirewallProfile, or sc stop mpssvc. Registry modifications under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\SharedAccess\Parameters\FirewallPolicy also indicate adversarial actions.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of admin tools and scripts allowed to legitimately modify firewall settings.
Number of firewall rule changes within a time window before triggering alert.

Detection Name: Detection of Data Destruction Across Platforms via Mass Overwrite and Deletion Patterns
Analysis Description: Adversary spawns command-line tools (e.g., del, cipher /w, SDelete) or scripts to recursively delete or overwrite user/system files. This may be correlated with abnormal file IO activity, registry writes, or tampering in critical system directories.

Mutable Elements.Description: Filter file deletion activity to sensitive locations (e.g., %System32%, Documents, DB paths).
Tune for aggressive overwrite flags (e.g., /w, /q, /s) or temp file overwrites.
Threshold of unique file deletions or modifications within time window.
Correlate rapid file delete/overwrite behavior from same process/user.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Forged SAML Tokens
Analysis Description: Forged SAML tokens may be used on Windows systems to authenticate to federated apps without normal Kerberos activity. Defenders may detect anomalous event correlation, where access to SaaS/O365 via SAML occurs without prior TGT requests or user logons.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:ADFS
Log Sources.Channel: Token issuance events showing anomalous claims or issuers

Mutable Elements.Description: Number of unusual claims in a SAML token (e.g., excessive privileges).

Detection Name: Detection of Exfiltration Over Unencrypted Non-C2 Protocol
Analysis Description: Detects data access or staging events followed by outbound data flows using unencrypted protocols (e.g., FTP, HTTP) initiated by unexpected processes or to rare destinations.

Mutable Elements.Description: Set of protocols considered suspicious for outbound data exfiltration (e.g., FTP, HTTP).
Defines what amount of outbound data is considered abnormal for a host/user.
Processes that should not launch FTP/HTTP clients (e.g., winword.exe launching ftp.exe).

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for File Creation or Modification of Boot Files
Analysis Description: Detection of raw access to physical drives, modification of boot records (MBR/VBR), and suspicious file creation or alteration within the EFI System Partition (ESP). Correlates privileged process execution with low-level disk modification and unexpected driver or firmware interactions.

Mutable Elements.Description: Baseline hashes of clean MBR/VBR sectors for comparison
Approved EFI executables within ESP directories
Correlation window between privileged access, raw disk modification, and EFI file creation

Detection Name: Behavior-chain, platform-aware detection strategy for T1124 System Time Discovery
Analysis Description: Untrusted or unusual process/script (cmd.exe, powershell.exe, w32tm.exe, net.exe, custom binaries) queries system time/timezone (e.g., w32tm /tz, net time \host, Get-TimeZone, GetTickCount API) and (optionally) is followed within a short window by time-based scheduling or conditional execution (e.g., schtasks /create, at.exe, PowerShell Start-Sleep with large values).

Log Sources.Name: etw:Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Process
Log Sources.Channel: High-frequency or suspicious sequence of QueryPerformanceCounter/GetTickCount API calls from a non-standard process lineage

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:TaskScheduler
Log Sources.Channel: Task registration/execution shortly after a time discovery event

Log Sources.Name: EDR:Telemetry
Log Sources.Channel: Process lineage and API usage enrichment (GetSystemTime, GetTimeZoneInformation, NtQuerySystemTime)

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlation window (e.g., 5–15 minutes) between time discovery and follow-on scheduling/conditional actions.
Legitimate parent processes (e.g., corporate scripts, management agents) that frequently call time APIs.
Extend/restrict keyword list for time queries (e.g., custom PS functions, .NET calls).
Restrict to non-service, non-administrative, or newly created/rare users.
Frequency threshold to exclude common estate-wide benign usage.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Exfiltration Over Webhook
Analysis Description: Unusual processes (e.g., powershell.exe, wscript.exe, mshta.exe) posting data to webhook endpoints (Discord, Slack, webhook.site) using HTTP POST/PUT requests. Defender perspective: suspicious process lineage followed by outbound HTTPS traffic to webhook domains.

Mutable Elements.Description: Domains to monitor such as discord.com/api/webhooks, slack.com/api, webhook.site.
Threshold for abnormal data sent via webhook requests.
List of approved business apps using webhooks to reduce noise.

Detection Name: Detect Screensaver-Based Persistence via Registry and Execution Chains
Analysis Description: Unusual screensaver (.scr) executions correlated with recent registry modifications to HKCU\Control Panel\Desktop values such as SCRNSAVE.exe, ScreenSaveTimeout, and ScreenSaveActive. Detection focuses on PE image paths not consistent with known legitimate screensavers and triggered after user inactivity timeout.

Mutable Elements.Description: Adjust the user inactivity threshold that defines 'screensaver trigger window'; shorter timeouts may increase sensitivity.
Allow tuning based on expected paths for legitimate .scr files vs suspicious locations (e.g., user temp directories).
Allowlisting known legitimate initiators of .scr files (e.g., user32.dll context) to reduce false positives.
Monitor for registry modification performed by unusual processes (e.g., powershell.exe, reg.exe).

Detection Name: Detect Kerberoasting Attempts (T1558.003)
Analysis Description: Detects Kerberoasting attempts by monitoring for anomalous Kerberos TGS requests (Event ID 4769) with RC4 encryption (etype 0x17), accounts requesting an unusual number of service tickets in a short period, or service accounts targeted outside normal usage baselines. Also correlates suspicious process activity (e.g., Mimikatz invoking LSASS access) with Kerberos ticket anomalies.

Mutable Elements.Description: Number of TGS requests per account within a defined window; higher than baseline may indicate Kerberoasting.
Permitted Kerberos encryption algorithms; RC4 (etype 0x17) usage in modern environments is suspicious.
Expected SPNs requested by specific accounts; anomalies may indicate adversarial targeting.
Correlation window for bursts of TGS requests; adjustable to reduce false positives.

Detection Name: Detection of Msiexec Abuse for Local, Network, and DLL Execution
Analysis Description: Detection of msiexec.exe execution where command-line arguments reference remote MSI packages, UNC paths, HTTP/HTTPS URLs, or DLLs, correlated with subsequent module loads and/or network connections to previously unseen destinations. The behavioral chain links process creation of msiexec.exe with suspicious parameters, network activity to retrieve payloads, and module loading indicative of malicious installation or DLL execution.

Mutable Elements.Description: Patterns for identifying malicious msiexec.exe usage (e.g., UNC paths, external domains, DLL execution flags)
List of external domains or IP ranges considered suspicious for msiexec network connections
Time range in seconds/minutes for correlating msiexec.exe execution with module load and network activity
Hash list of MSI packages considered known-good to reduce false positives

Detection Name: Detect Remote Access via USB Hardware (TinyPilot, PiKVM)
Analysis Description: Detection of USB-based remote access hardware (e.g., TinyPilot, PiKVM) attached to the host via drive or peripheral enumeration, triggering vendor identifiers or unusual EDID announcements.

Mutable Elements.Description: Device vendor strings may need tuning to include additional remote hardware sources.
Serial numbers for known implants can vary per campaign and may need expansion.
Adjust the detection window for peripheral enumeration based on environment and operating hours.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Multi-Factor Authentication Request Generation (T1621)
Analysis Description: Detect repeated failed login events followed by MFA challenges triggered in rapid succession, especially if originating from service accounts or anomalous IP addresses.

Mutable Elements.Description: Exclude specific accounts where automated MFA requests are legitimate

Detection Name: Password Policy Discovery – cross-platform behavior-chain analytics
Analysis Description: Cause→effect chain: (1) a user or service spawns a shell/PowerShell that queries local/domain password policy via commands/cmdlets (e.g., net accounts, Get-ADDefaultDomainPasswordPolicy, secedit /export); (2) optional directory/LDAP reads from DCs; (3) same principal performs adjacent Discovery or credential-related actions within a short window. Correlate sysmon process creation with PowerShell ScriptBlock and Security logs.

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlation window between policy query and adjacent suspicious activity (e.g., 15–30 minutes).
Accounts (e.g., Helpdesk) allowed to run policy queries routinely.
Limit alerts on DCs/management servers; raise on user workstations/VDI.
Cmdlet/function names to treat as high-signal in your environment.

Detection Name: Socket-filter trigger → on-host raw-socket activity → reverse connection (T1205.002)
Analysis Description: Adversary installs/uses packet-capture or raw-socket capability (WinPcap/Npcap, wpcap/packet DLLs or raw socket attach) and sets a filter. A crafted inbound packet is observed; within a short window the host process that loaded capture libraries initiates an outbound connection (e.g., reverse shell) to the packet origin.

Log Sources.Name: NSM:Flow
Log Sources.Channel: Single, low-volume inbound packet (REJ/S0/OTH or uncommon dport/protocol) from src_ip followed by outbound SF connection to src_ip.

Mutable Elements.Description: Seconds to correlate inbound trigger → process library load/driver start → outbound connect (default 120s).
DLL/driver names to match (wpcap.dll, packet.dll, npcap.sys, npf.sys) – extend for EDR drivers in your fleet.
Signed/expected processes allowed to install/start Npcap (software distribution tools).
Likely egress ports to watch after trigger (4444, 53, 80/443, 8080, high ephemeral).

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Command History Clearing
Analysis Description: Detects PowerShell Clear-History invocation or deletion of ConsoleHost_history.txt to erase past PowerShell session history.

Mutable Elements.Description: Path to PSReadLine file, typically in APPDATA.
User account or role performing deletion (e.g., low-priv user deleting history).
Support detection of `Clear-History` and variations.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for T1505.002 - Transport Agent Abuse (Windows/Linux)
Analysis Description: Adversary registers a malicious Microsoft Exchange transport agent DLL (.NET assembly), configures it via PowerShell or Exchange Management Shell, and persists code execution by manipulating email processing logic based on rules or headers.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Application
Log Sources.Channel: Exchange Transport Service loads unusual .NET assembly or errors upon transport agent execution

Mutable Elements.Description: May need tuning based on frequency of Exchange agent updates in environment.
Specific DLL paths used by Exchange for registered agents may vary between deployments.
Tunable threshold for repeated use of transport agent management cmdlets.

Detection Name: Firmware Modification via Flash Tool or Corrupted Firmware Upload
Analysis Description: Firmware flash utility invoked with elevated privileges followed by raw access to firmware device path or changes to boot configuration.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Boot
Log Sources.Channel: Firmware integrity validation failed or boot configuration tampered

Mutable Elements.Description: Common legitimate flash tool chains can be allowlisted
Flags indicating silent or forced flash may vary

Detection Name: Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion via System Checks across Windows, Linux, macOS
Analysis Description: Script or binary performs a rapid sequence of system discovery checks (e.g., CPU count, RAM size, registry keys, running processes) indicative of VM detection

Mutable Elements.Description: Sequence of system enumeration events within X seconds
Parent-child lineage to identify potentially suspicious launch sources (e.g., Office, browser, WMI, PowerShell)
Limit to non-admin or interactive sessions if desired

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Forged Web Cookies
Analysis Description: Forged web cookies on Windows endpoints can be detected by monitoring unusual modifications of browser cookie stores (e.g., Chrome SQLite DB, Edge cache) by processes outside of browsers, followed by authentication events to SaaS or IaaS services. Defenders may observe processes writing directly to cookie storage paths or injecting tokens into browser sessions.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of monitored cookie file paths on Windows systems.
Approved processes allowed to write to browser cookie stores.

Detection Name: Behavior-chain, platform-aware detection strategy for T1127 Trusted Developer Utilities Proxy Execution (Windows)
Analysis Description: A trusted/signed developer utility (parent) is executed in a non-developer context and (a) spawns suspicious children (e.g., powershell.exe, cmd.exe, rundll32.exe, regsvr32.exe, wscript.exe), (b) loads unsigned/user-writable DLLs, (c) writes and then runs a new PE from user-writable paths, and/or (d) immediately makes outbound network connections.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:AppLocker
Log Sources.Channel: AppLocker audit/blocks showing developer utilities executing scripts/binaries outside policy

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlation window between developer utility execution, payload write, and network egress (e.g., 0–30 minutes).
Org-specific list of dev utilities legitimately used on build/dev hosts to suppress noise.
List of known developer/build systems where these tools are expected; raise severity off-host.
Child processes considered high-risk when spawned by dev utilities (powershell.exe, rundll32.exe, regsvr32.exe, cmd.exe, wscript.exe, mshta.exe).
Regex of user-writable or atypical paths (e.g., %TEMP%, %APPDATA%, recycle bin, public profile) for payload drops.
Toggle to alert only when child/payload is unsigned or signature invalid to reduce noise.
Known orchestrators (e.g., CI/CD agents) that often run these utilities legitimately.
Heuristic for rare/unknown destination (no DNS reputation, new domain, geo outside region).

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Endpoint DoS via Service Exhaustion Flood
Analysis Description: High-frequency, repetitive service requests (e.g., HTTP, TLS renegotiation) originating from a single or small set of source IPs targeting endpoint web services or application ports, leading to exhaustion of CPU or memory on targeted Windows services.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Application
Log Sources.Channel: Unexpected spikes in request volume, application-level errors, or thread pool exhaustion in web or API logs

Log Sources.Name: Windows:perfmon
Log Sources.Channel: Sustained CPU/memory exhaustion by service process (e.g., w3wp.exe)

Mutable Elements.Description: Defines burst threshold (e.g., 1 min, 5 min) for connection spikes
Specific ports/services likely to be abused (e.g., 80, 443, 8080)
Level of sustained CPU usage considered anomalous for a given service

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Exploitation for Credential Access
Analysis Description: Detects adversary exploitation of authentication mechanisms or credential validation processes. Defender perspective includes forged Kerberos tickets (e.g., MS14-068), abnormal LSASS memory access, replayed authentication attempts, and unexpected crashes of authentication services. Multi-event correlation ties exploitation attempts to abnormal process creation, service instability, and suspicious authentication events.

Mutable Elements.Description: High-value accounts (e.g., Domain Admins) for anomalous ticket issuance or replay activity.
Time window for correlating duplicate or replayed Kerberos authentications.

Detection Name: Drive-by Compromise — Behavior-based, Multi-platform Detection Strategy (T1189)
Analysis Description: Correlated evidence of anomalous browser/network behavior (suspicious external resource fetches and script injection patterns) followed by atypical child processes, ephemeral execution contexts, memory modification or process injection, and unexpected file drops. Defender sees network requests to previously unseen/suspicious domains or resources + browser process spawning unusual children or loading unsigned modules + file writes or registry changes shortly after those requests.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Application
Log Sources.Channel: Browser or plugin/application logs showing script errors, plugin enumerations, or unusual extension load events

Log Sources.Name: etw:Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Process

Log Sources.Name: NSM:Flow
Log Sources.Channel: http.request: HTTP requests and responses for specific script resources, unexpected content-types (application/octet-stream for script URLs), suspicious referrers, or obfuscated javascript resources

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlation time window between suspicious network fetch and subsequent process/file events. Tweak for environment latency and caching; default 2 minutes.
Allowlist of high-volume, benign domains used by corporate sites or CDNs to reduce false positives.
Entropy threshold for downloaded script/binary content to surface likely obfuscated/packed payloads.
Exclude or treat differently known administrative service accounts or build machines versus end-user contexts.

Detection Name: Detect Persistence via Outlook Home Page Exploitation
Analysis Description: Adversary uses a tool like Ruler to configure a malicious Outlook folder Home Page that loads a remote or embedded HTML payload upon folder interaction. Execution chain begins with Outlook launching, a specific folder being accessed, and a suspicious child process being spawned or COM-based execution invoked.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Application
Log Sources.Channel: Outlook logs indicating failure to load or render HTML page in Home Page view

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:PowerShell
Log Sources.Channel: Execution of PowerShell script to enumerate or remove malicious Home Page folder config

The Home Page URL may point to internal or external content, hosted on trusted or unknown domains
Execution may result in launch of scripting hosts (e.g., mshta.exe, wscript.exe) from outlook.exe
Execution may occur only when the specific folder is accessed after launch, not immediately at startup
Behavior may vary if the folder's form view is customized or suppressed via GPO

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Unauthorized VNC Remote Control Sessions
Analysis Description: Detection of VNC service or executable starting unexpectedly, followed by user session creation and interactive desktop activity (mouse/keyboard simulation).

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlate VNC process with user logon activity within defined time span
Trackable VNC executable names (e.g., vncserver.exe, winvnc.exe)
Limit detection to interactive logons (type 10)

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Permission Groups Discovery
Analysis Description: Detection of adversary enumeration of domain or local group memberships via native tools such as net.exe, PowerShell, or WMI. This activity may precede lateral movement or privilege escalation.

Mutable Elements.Description: Regex filters for matching suspicious group enumeration commands (e.g., 'net group', 'Get-ADGroupMember').
Time threshold for correlating group discovery with subsequent suspicious activity (e.g., lateral movement).
Whether the user performing discovery is in a sensitive group or running under unusual context (e.g., non-admin querying Domain Admins).

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for T1547.009 – Shortcut Modification (Windows)
Analysis Description: Detection correlates file creation or modification of .lnk (shortcut) files in autostart locations with anomalous parent-child process lineage or unsigned binaries. Defenders should watch for LNK creation/modification events outside of known software installations, patch events, or OS updates. Flag shortcut targets pointing to suspicious locations or unknown binaries, particularly those written by script interpreters or spawned from phishing delivery chains.

Mutable Elements.Description: Tunable regex to flag suspicious shortcut target paths (e.g., temp folder, base64 in target, unusual executable names)
Time window used to correlate shortcut creation with process execution (e.g., 5-minute window)
Filter for expected administrative installs versus end-user initiated shortcut creation
Configurable value to filter LNK files tagged with external source markers (e.g., ZoneId=3 for Internet)

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for SQL Stored Procedures Abuse via T1505.001
Analysis Description: Creation or modification of stored procedures invoking xp_cmdshell or CLR assemblies for command execution and persistence.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Application
Log Sources.Channel: Stored procedure creation, modification, or xp_cmdshell invocation via SQL logs or SQL Server auditing

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Application
Log Sources.Channel: CLR Assembly creation, loading, or modification logs via MSSQL CLR integration

Organization-defined whitelist of legitimate CLR assemblies
Tune time window to correlate stored procedure creation with process execution

Detection Name: Behavior-chain detection for T1135 Network Share Discovery across Windows, Linux, and macOS
Analysis Description: Process or script enumerates network shares via CLI (net view/net share, PowerShell Get-SmbShare/WMI) or OS APIs (NetShareEnum/ srvsvc.NetShareEnumAll RPC) → bursts of outbound SMB/RPC connections (445/139, \host\IPC$ / srvsvc) to many hosts inside a short window → optional follow-on file listing or copy operations.

Log Sources.Channel: rpc_call: srvsvc.NetShareEnum / NetShareEnumAll from non-admin or unusual processes

Correlation window between the discovery process start and SMB fan-out (default 10m).
Service/admin accounts legitimately running inventory scripts.
Pipes (e.g., \PIPE\spoolss) normally accessed by management agents; exclude from alerts.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Lateral Tool Transfer across OS platforms
Analysis Description: Correlate suspicious file transfers over SMB or Admin$ shares with process creation events (e.g., cmd.exe, powershell.exe, certutil.exe) that do not align with normal administrative behavior. Detect remote file writes followed by execution of transferred binaries.
