Exclude known legitimate software update directories

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Indicator Removal Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Monitors sequences involving deletion/modification of logs, registry keys, scheduled tasks, or prefetch files following suspicious process activity or elevated access escalation.

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlate indicator removal within X mins after persistence/setup activities
Customize detection to log file paths or common registry hives

Detection Name: Automated File and API Collection Detection Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Automated execution of native utilities and scripts to discover, enumerate, and exfiltrate files and clipboard content. Focus is on detecting repeated file access, scripting engine use, and use of command-line utilities commonly leveraged by collection scripts.

Mutable Elements.Description: Defines the lookback period for identifying burst activity or patterns in process/file events.
Tunable list of file extensions associated with collection (e.g., .pdf, .docx).
The number of times a process executes before considered anomalous.

Detection Name: Detect disabled Windows event logging
Analysis Description: Detection of attempts to disable or tamper with Windows Event Logging. This includes stopping or disabling the EventLog service, modifying registry keys related to EventLog and Autologger, using auditpol or wevtutil to disable categories or clear audit policies, and detecting suspicious gaps or resets in event logs. Defenders observe registry changes, service state changes, process execution of disabling commands, and anomalies in event record sequences.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of accounts authorized to legitimately modify audit policies or disable services.
Correlation window between registry modification, service stop, and audit policy commands.
Customizable set of monitored services such as EventLog, Sysmon, or custom loggers.

Detection Name: Local Storage Discovery via Drive Enumeration and Filesystem Probing
Analysis Description: Drive enumeration using PowerShell (Get-PSDrive), wmic logicaldisk, or Win32 API indicative of local volume enumeration by non-admin users or executed outside of baseline system inventory scripts.

Mutable Elements.Description: Non-system accounts performing drive enumeration may be higher fidelity indicators
Baseline parent-child process lineage can help distinguish admin tools from malicious scripts

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Indicator Removal from Tools - Post-AV Evasion Modification
Analysis Description: Detection of known tools or malware flagged by antivirus, followed by a near-term drop of a similar binary with modified signature and resumed activity (execution, C2, or persistence).

Mutable Elements.Description: Vendor-specific signature string or detection message that can be correlated to threat intel context.
The time between AV alert and similar file/process activity (e.g., 5–30 minutes)
String or hash similarity thresholds between original and modified binary.

Detection Name: Detect MFA Modification or Disabling Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Detects registry and Group Policy modifications that disable or weaken MFA, suspicious PowerShell usage modifying MFA-related attributes, and anomalous login sessions succeeding without expected MFA challenge.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:PowerShell
Log Sources.Channel: Set-ADUser or Set-ADAuthenticationPolicy with MFA attributes disabled

Mutable Elements.Description: List of AD attributes or policy fields tied to MFA enforcement that may vary by organization.
Correlation window between MFA policy changes and anomalous login behavior.

Detection Name: Behavior-chain detection strategy for T1127.002 Trusted Developer Utilities Proxy Execution: ClickOnce (Windows)
Analysis Description: Abuse of ClickOnce applications where rundll32.exe invokes dfshim.dll with ShOpenVerbApplication or dfsvc.exe spawns unexpected child processes or loads unsigned modules.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-Security-Mitigations/KernelMode
Log Sources.Channel: ETW telemetry indicating ClickOnce deployment (dfsvc.exe) launching payloads

Mutable Elements.Description: The correlation window for dfsvc.exe/rundll32.exe execution and subsequent module loads or child processes (e.g., 0–10 minutes).
Whitelist of legitimate ClickOnce applications and paths.
Child processes considered abnormal when launched by dfsvc.exe or rundll32.exe.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Email Hiding Rules
Analysis Description: Suspicious creation or modification of inbox rules through PowerShell (New-InboxRule, Set-InboxRule) to automatically delete, move, or hide emails. Defender perspective: unusual rule activity correlated with mailbox access and filtering patterns.

Log Sources.Name: m365:unified
Log Sources.Channel: New-InboxRule or Set-InboxRule events recorded in Exchange Online

Mutable Elements.Description: Keywords like 'phish', 'malware', 'suspicious' used in inbox rules to hide emails.
Scope mailbox monitoring to high-value users such as executives or admins.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Stored Data Manipulation across OS Platforms.
Analysis Description: Identify unauthorized creation, deletion, or modification of business-critical stored data such as Office documents, database files, and log archives. Detect anomalous processes modifying stored data outside of expected workflows (e.g., non-database processes modifying database files).

Mutable Elements.Description: Paths to sensitive stored data files such as database directories or email archives.
List of legitimate processes expected to create, delete, or modify stored data.
Threshold for correlating multiple suspicious file operations within a short period.

Detection Name: Detection of Malicious Control Panel Item Execution via control.exe or Rundll32
Analysis Description: Execution of control.exe or rundll32.exe with parameters pointing to CPL files, especially from non-standard directories or newly created files, followed by suspicious child process execution or registry modifications registering new Control Panel items.

Mutable Elements.Description: Regex to match CPL file paths; tune to exclude legitimate CPLs in System32
Helps filter known parent processes that legitimately use control.exe
Time delta between CPL file creation and execution to detect rapid execution of newly dropped files
Whitelist of known good CPL registry entries

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of System Network Configuration Discovery
Analysis Description: Execution of built-in tools (e.g., ipconfig, route, netsh) or PowerShell/WMI queries to enumerate IP, MAC, interface status, or routing configuration.

Mutable Elements.Description: Filter known/legit CLI chains (e.g., explorer.exe → cmd.exe) to reduce FP
Target executions by non-admin or unexpected users
Cluster enumeration commands within short time windows

Detection Name: Domain Fronting Behavior via Mismatched TLS SNI and HTTP Host Headers
Analysis Description: Suspicious outbound HTTPS connections where the TLS Server Name Indication (SNI) does not match the HTTP Host header, indicating potential use of domain fronting to mask C2 traffic via CDNs.

Mutable Elements.Description: Define acceptable mismatch ratio between SNI and HTTP Host fields based on legitimate domain usage patterns.
Whitelist of known safe CDN front-end domains (e.g., `cdn.company.com`).
Filter for suspicious initiators of domain fronting, e.g., scripting engines, lolbins, unknown binaries.

Detection Name: Behavior-chain, platform-aware detection strategy for T1125 Video Capture
Analysis Description: A non-standard process (or script-hosted process) loads camera/video-capture libraries (e.g., avicap32.dll, mf.dll, ksproxy.ax), opens the Camera Frame Server/device, writes video/image artifacts (e.g., .mp4/.avi/.yuv) to unusual locations, and optionally initiates outbound transfer shortly after.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-Windows Camera Frame Server/Operational
Log Sources.Channel: Process session start/stop events for camera pipeline by unexpected executables

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlation window (e.g., 0–20 minutes) between device access, file creation, and egress.
Known legitimate camera consumers (e.g., Teams.exe, zoom.exe, obs64.exe) to suppress.
List of extensions to flag (.mp4, .avi, .mov, .yuv, .mkv, .h264) – tune for your estate.
Regex for unusual storage locations (e.g., %TEMP%\*, C:\Windows\Tasks\*, user profile hidden dirs).
Minimum size to reduce FP from thumbnails/snapshots.
Service/agent parents permitted to broker camera access.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Virtual Machine Discovery
Analysis Description: Detects enumeration of VMs using PowerShell (Get-VM), VMware Workstation (vmrun.exe), or Hyper-V (VBoxManage.exe). Defender observes suspicious command lines executed by unexpected users or outside normal administrative sessions.

Mutable Elements.Description: Defines which accounts are authorized to execute VM discovery commands.
Whitelist of approved administrative scripts that legitimately invoke VM enumeration.

Detection Name: Indirect Command Execution – Windows utility abuse behavior chain
Analysis Description: Cause→effect chain: (1) A user or service launches an indirection utility (e.g., forfiles.exe, pcalua.exe, wsl.exe, scriptrunner.exe, ssh.exe with -o ProxyCommand/LocalCommand). (2) That utility spawns a secondary program/command (PowerShell, cmd, msiexec, regsvr32, curl, arbitrary EXE) and/or opens outbound network connections. (3) Optional precursor modification of SSH config to persist LocalCommand/ProxyCommand. Correlate process creation, command/script content, file access to %USERPROFILE%.ssh\config, and network connections from the utility or its child.

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlation window between indirect launcher and spawned child/network activity (e.g., 10–30 minutes).
Utilities permitted on admin/Jumphosts (forfiles, wsl, ssh) to reduce noise.
Child images that indicate abuse (powershell.exe, cmd.exe, rundll32.exe, regsvr32.exe, mshta.exe, msiexec.exe, curl.exe, bitsadmin.exe).
Raise severity when the actor is a standard/interactive user on a workstation rather than a server or CI agent.
Known-good egress networks for SSH/WSL activity to suppress expected admin automations.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Hijack Execution Flow for DLLs
Analysis Description: DLL hijacking behaviors including unexpected DLL loads from non-standard directories, replacement of DLLs, phantom DLL insertion, redirection file creation, and substitution of legitimate DLLs. Defender correlates file system modifications, registry changes, and module load telemetry to detect abnormal DLL behavior in trusted processes.

Mutable Elements.Description: Known safe DLL directories to suppress false positives (e.g., C:\Windows\System32).
Applications expected to load DLLs from non-standard locations (e.g., development tools).
Correlation interval between DLL file creation, registry changes, and module load.
Baseline hashes for legitimate DLLs used to detect substitution.

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Windows Command Shell Execution
Analysis Description: Detects interactive or scripted abuse of cmd.exe, batch files, or shell invocation chains. Focuses on parent-child relationships (e.g., cmd.exe launched from unusual parents), anomalous command-line parameters, and chaining with discovery, credential access, or lateral movement behaviors.

Mutable Elements.Description: Cmd.exe launched from uncommon parents (e.g., msedge.exe, winword.exe) may indicate abuse.
Cmd or .bat execution during non-working hours may indicate automation or C2 activity.
Flags suspicious switches (e.g., /c ping, /k whoami) or command chaining (&&, ^).
Batch file execution from %TEMP%, C:\Users\Public, or external drives.
Flags admin-level users executing cmd outside expected baselines.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for T1547.010 – Port Monitor DLL Persistence via spoolsv.exe (Windows)
Analysis Description: Detects suspicious registry modifications under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Print\Monitors\*\Driver, DLL loads by spoolsv.exe of non-standard or unsigned modules, and abnormal usage of the AddMonitor API by non-installation processes. This pattern often indicates an attempt to persist a malicious DLL via the print monitor mechanism, particularly when correlated with creation of files in C:\Windows\System32 not tied to known patches or installations.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Application
Log Sources.Channel: API call to AddMonitor invoked by non-installer process

Mutable Elements.Description: Expected directory path for legitimate monitor DLLs (e.g., C:\Windows\System32)
Enable/disable signature validation on DLLs loaded by spoolsv.exe
Define whether only SYSTEM/user installs are expected to make changes to the port monitor registry keys
Timeframe between registry modification and subsequent spoolsv.exe DLL load
Filter on calling process of AddMonitor API to detect anomalies outside installer/updater

Detection Name: Detect XSL Script Abuse via msxsl and wmic
Analysis Description: Execution of XSL scripts via msxsl.exe or wmic.exe using embedded JScript or VBScript for proxy execution. Detection correlates process creation, command-line patterns, and module load behavior of scripting components (e.g., jscript.dll).

Mutable Elements.Description: May need to tune based on encoded input or custom extensions (e.g., .jpeg instead of .xsl).
Legitimate administrative or developer tools may use msxsl; validate the parent process chain.
Temporal correlation window between script engine DLL load and suspicious process spawn.
Filter known safe URLs used by enterprise for XSL transformations.

Detection Name: Detect LSA Authentication Package Persistence via Registry and LSASS DLL Load
Analysis Description: Registry modification of the LSA Authentication Packages key followed by LSASS loading a non-standard or unsigned DLL. This includes unusual write access to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa, especially during non-installation timeframes. Correlated with lsass.exe loading DLLs not present in baseline or lacking valid signatures.

Mutable Elements.Description: Time between registry write and DLL load; tune based on reboot cycles or scheduled maintenance
Allow listing of known signed LSASS-authenticated DLLs versus unknown/untrusted ones
Allow tuning for subkeys beyond just `Authentication Packages` (e.g., `Security Packages`, `Notification Packages`)
Correlate user responsible for registry edit; tune for expected administrative/service accounts
Validate process lineage for registry modification; expected tools like `reg.exe` or `powershell.exe`

Detection Name: Endpoint Resource Saturation and Crash Pattern Detection Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Excessive resource exhaustion or service crash induced by processes launched by users or scripts that rapidly consume CPU/memory or attempt malformed service interactions.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Application
Log Sources.Channel: Service crash, unhandled exception, or application hang warnings for critical services (e.g., IIS, DNS, SQL Server)

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:System
Log Sources.Channel: System shutdowns due to bugcheck (Event ID 1001) or watchdog timer expirations

Mutable Elements.Description: Number of service crashes or high-CPU events within a defined time period
Specific service name or executable targeted for DoS (e.g., svchost.exe, w3wp.exe)
CPU usage percent considered anomalous over duration

Detection Name: Detection of Registry Query for Environmental Discovery
Analysis Description: Registry read access associated with suspicious or non-interactive processes querying system config, installed software, or security settings.

Mutable Elements.Description: Focus detection on registry hives or keys likely to reveal environment info (e.g., HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion).
May tune for suspicious parent processes such as cmd.exe, wscript.exe, or mshta.exe.
Controls how closely registry access must follow process creation for correlation.

Detection Name: Abuse of Domain Accounts
Analysis Description: Detection of suspicious logon behavior using valid domain accounts across multiple hosts, off-hours, or simultaneous sessions from geographically distant locations.

Mutable Elements.Description: Tune for detection of off-hours or abnormal logon spikes.
Scope to sensitive domain accounts (e.g., Domain Admins).
Distinguish between interactive, service, and network logons.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for T1505.005 – Terminal Services DLL Modification (Windows)
Analysis Description: Adversary modifies or replaces the Terminal Services DLL (termsrv.dll) or changes the associated ServiceDll Registry value to load an arbitrary or patched DLL that enables persistent and enhanced RDP access. This may include binary replacement, registry tampering, and unexpected module loads by the svchost.exe -k termsvcs process.

Mutable Elements.Description: Defenders may tune for non-standard DLLs loaded by svchost.exe or termsrv.exe processes.
Environment-specific variations in the path to `ServiceDll` registry key (e.g., nested group policies).
Correlation time window for registry change followed by DLL load or svchost restart.
Some environments may spawn registry changes from automation tools or administrative scripts.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Data Transfer Size Limits and Chunked Exfiltration
Analysis Description: Adversary uses a process to establish outbound connections that transmit uniform packet sizes at a consistent interval, avoiding threshold-based network alerts.

Mutable Elements.Description: Minimum repetitive size in bytes to consider as anomalous behavior (e.g., 512B or 1024B)
Timeframe over which repeated, evenly spaced transfers are flagged
Common ports expected to exhibit protocol behavior; outliers flagged if not matching expected usage

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Embedded Payloads
Analysis Description: Detection of executables or scripts containing hidden embedded resources or secondary payloads, often with anomalies in file size vs. functionality or dropped child binaries.

Mutable Elements.Description: Threshold in bytes where appended sections to binaries are considered suspicious
Controls how far child process lineage is analyzed for dropped embedded payloads
Defines correlation interval between file write and process execution

Detection Name: Detection of Multi-Platform File Encryption for Impact
Analysis Description: High-frequency file write operations using uncommon extensions, followed by ransom note creation, registry tampering, or shadow copy deletion. Often uses CLI tools like vssadmin, wbadmin, cipher, or PowerShell.

Mutable Elements.Description: Non-standard or randomly generated file extensions may indicate encrypted content.
Focus on user document folders, network shares, or system paths like %System32%.
Correlate rapid writes and renames within seconds across high file count.
Flag common ransomware tools or functions (vssadmin delete shadows /all /quiet).

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Extra Window Memory (EWM) Injection on Windows
Analysis Description: Detects adversary manipulation of Extra Window Memory (EWM) in a GUI process, where the attacker uses SetWindowLong or SetClassLong to redirect function pointers to injected shellcode stored in shared memory, then triggers execution via a window message like SendNotifyMessage.

Mutable Elements.Description: Regex to scope suspicious or uncommon GUI class names registered by user-created processes
API calls like SendNotifyMessage or PostMessage that deliver execution to the shellcode location
Set byte count thresholds on suspicious memory writes to known shared sections
Define max time (e.g., <10s) between API call to set window memory and the message call to trigger it

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Hijack Execution Flow across OS platforms.
Analysis Description: Unusual modifications to service binary paths, registry keys, or DLL load paths resulting in alternate execution flow. Defender observes registry key modifications, suspicious file writes into system directories, and processes loading libraries from abnormal paths.

Mutable Elements.Description: Expected registry keys and service paths for comparison.
Directories considered valid for DLL loading.
Correlation interval between registry/file modification and process execution.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Escape to Host
Analysis Description: Detection of Windows container escape attempts by observing processes accessing host directories, symbolic link abuse, or privilege escalation attempts. Defenders may detect anomalous process execution with access to system-level directories outside of container boundaries.

Mutable Elements.Description: Critical system paths containers should not access (e.g., C:\Windows, C:\ProgramData).

Detection Name: Detection of USB-Based Data Exfiltration
Analysis Description: Detects USB device insertion followed by high-volume or sensitive file access and staging activity by suspicious processes or accounts.

Mutable Elements.Description: Match data staging or export paths (e.g., *.docx, *.csv, *.db) to USB volume letters.
Limit to users who do not normally use removable devices (e.g., service accounts).
Correlate events within a short period following USB insert (e.g., 5–10 minutes).

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection Strategy for T1123 Audio Capture Across Windows, Linux, macOS
Analysis Description: Unusual or unauthorized processes accessing microphone APIs (e.g., winmm.dll, avrt.dll) followed by audio file writes to user-accessible or temp directories.

Mutable Elements.Description: Time span in which the process accesses audio APIs and writes files, to reduce false positives.
Set of approved processes known to legitimately use microphone (e.g., Zoom, Teams).
Allowlist of paths where legitimate apps store audio (e.g., user media folders).

Detection Name: Detecting MMC (.msc) Proxy Execution and Malicious COM Activation
Analysis Description: Abuse of mmc.exe to execute non-Microsoft or user-staged .msc files and malicious COM CLSIDs. Behavioral chain: (1) suspicious mmc.exe invocation with /a or -Embedding and non-standard .msc path → (2) COM activation of non-baseline CLSIDs by mmc.exe → (3) mmc.exe loads non-baseline DLLs (user-writable/UNC/unsigned) → (4) optional network/DNS activity from mmc.exe.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-COM/Operational
Log Sources.Channel: CLSID activation events where ProcessName=mmc.exe and CLSID not in allowed baseline

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlation window (e.g., 5–10 minutes) tying .msc creation → mmc.exe start → module loads → COM/net activity.
Set of Microsoft-supplied .msc names/paths allowed in the environment to suppress noise.
Regex for user-writable and network paths indicating risky .msc staging (Users, AppData, Downloads, Desktop, UNC).
Baseline of CLSIDs expected to be activated by mmc.exe; alert on unknown/new.
Expected parents for mmc.exe (explorer.exe, services) vs. unusual (powershell, wscript, office apps).
Flag when signed mmc.exe results in loading unsigned DLLs.
Approved external ranges/domains to exclude when mmc.exe makes network requests.

Detection Name: Detection of Adversary Abuse of Software Deployment Tools
Analysis Description: Detects SCCM, Intune, or remote push execution spawning scripts or binaries from SYSTEM context or unusual consoles (e.g., cmtrace.exe launching PowerShell or cmd.exe).

Mutable Elements.Description: Allowlist of known SCCM-related binary spawners (e.g., 'CCMExec.exe')
Expected deployment activity from scheduled system accounts
Unusual deployment timing outside standard maintenance hours

Detection Name: Detect Abuse of Component Object Model (T1559.001)
Analysis Description: Detects anomalous use of COM objects for execution, such as Office applications spawning scripting engines, enumeration of COM interfaces via registry queries, or processes loading atypical DLLs through COM activation. Correlates process creation, module loads, and registry queries to flag suspicious COM-based code execution or persistence.

Mutable Elements.Description: Legitimate COM CLSIDs and ProgIDs used by enterprise applications, to reduce false positives.
Expected parent-child process relationships (e.g., explorer.exe spawning dllhost.exe).
Threshold for correlating COM object execution with subsequent process creation or DLL load.

Detection Name: Detect unauthorized LSASS driver persistence via LSA plugin abuse (Windows)
Analysis Description: Unauthorized creation or modification of DLLs loaded by LSASS, abnormal registry values under LSA extensions, and anomalous DLL load activity into the lsass.exe process context—correlated during boot or logon events.

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlate DLL file creation/modification with LSASS execution within a configurable timeframe (e.g., 5 min)
Tune based on known legitimate LSASS plugin DLL paths
Flag unsigned DLLs loaded into lsass.exe or those signed by unexpected publishers
Scope to specific registry keys: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\Authentication Packages
Exclude known-good LSASS plugin DLLs based on cryptographic hash

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Masquerading via File Type Modification
Analysis Description: Detects behavior where files with non-executable or misleading extensions (e.g., .jpg, .txt) are created or modified but subsequently executed as binaries based on internal file headers or abnormal parent process lineage. This includes identifying polyglot files or malformed magic bytes indicative of masquerading attempts.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of non-executable file types commonly used to mask payloads (.jpg, .txt, .gif)
Targeted directories for initial access and downloads (e.g., %TEMP%, Downloads, AppData)
Detection tolerance for mismatches between extension and file signature (magic bytes)
Time range between file creation and first execution
Anomaly score threshold for suspicious parent-child process combinations

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Non-Standard Ports
Analysis Description: Processes initiating outbound connections on uncommon ports or using protocols inconsistent with the assigned port. Correlating process creation with subsequent network connections reveals anomalies such as svchost.exe or Office applications using high, atypical ports.

Mutable Elements.Description: Define what constitutes a 'non-standard port' based on organizational baselines (e.g., allow 443/80/22 but flag 8088/587/3389 changes).
Processes normally allowed to use non-standard ports (e.g., custom apps).
Correlate process creation and network activity within N seconds.

Detection Name: Detect Multi-Stage Command and Control Channels
Analysis Description: Initial process initiates outbound connection to first-stage C2, receives payloads or commands, then spawns or injects into a second process that establishes a new outbound connection to an unrelated destination (second-stage C2).

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlate two-stage behavior occurring within a short window (e.g., 1-5 minutes)
Tune to exclude known legitimate updaters and management agents
May be customized to exclude known corporate domains and CDNs

Detection Name: Enumeration of Global Address Lists via Email Account Discovery
Analysis Description: Enumeration of global address lists or email account metadata via PowerShell cmdlets (e.g., Get-GlobalAddressList) or MAPI/RPC from non-admin, non-mailserver systems.

Mutable Elements.Description: Match variations of Get-GlobalAddressList, Get-Recipient, and related cmdlets.
Suppress expected usage on Exchange servers or known IT admin consoles.
Detect bulk execution patterns in short intervals, often used during recon.

Detection Name: Detect Suspicious or Malicious Code Signing Abuse
Analysis Description: Detects execution of binaries signed with unusual or recently issued certificates, correlation of process execution with abnormal publisher metadata, and mismatched certificate chains. Monitors for revoked or unknown code signing certificates used in high-privilege contexts.

Mutable Elements.Description: Define trusted issuers to suppress noise from legitimate enterprise signing chains
Correlation window for detecting execution of binaries with newly observed or anomalous certificates
Baseline normal age of certificates; flag very recent or expired certificates

Detection Name: Credential Dumping via Sensitive Memory and Registry Access Correlation
Analysis Description: Processes accessing LSASS memory or SAM registry hives outside of trusted security tools, often followed by file creation or lateral movement. Detects unauthorized access to sensitive OS subsystems for credential extraction.

Mutable Elements.Description: Set to detect full access rights (0x1F0FFF) or modify based on tool behavior.
Define how soon access to LSASS is followed by suspicious file or registry activity.
Allowlist known security tools or system processes accessing LSASS.

Detection Name: Detecting Steganographic Command and Control via File + Network Correlation
Analysis Description: Detect the creation or modification of common media file formats (e.g., .jpg, .png, .wav) following suspicious process activity like compression or encryption, especially when paired with lateral movement or exfiltration behavior.

Mutable Elements.Description: Allows tuning of monitored file types (e.g., .jpg, .png, .docx).
Threshold for flagging potential hidden data in outbound payloads.
Time window between media creation and network transmission.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Spearphishing Attachment across OS Platforms
Analysis Description: Detection of spearphishing attachments by correlating suspicious email delivery with subsequent file creation and abnormal process execution (e.g., Office spawning PowerShell or CMD). Behavior chain includes inbound email metadata → attachment stored on disk → process execution → outbound network activity.

Log Sources.Name: m365:unified
Log Sources.Channel: Send/Receive: Inbound emails with attachments from suspicious or spoofed senders

Mutable Elements.Description: List of high-risk extensions to monitor (e.g., .exe, .js, .vbs, .docm, .xlsm).
Process lineage patterns considered malicious (e.g., winword.exe → powershell.exe).
Correlation window between email receipt, file creation, and process execution.

Detection Name: Defacement via File and Web Content Modification Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Adversary modifies website or application-hosted content via unauthorized file changes or script injections, often by exploiting web servers or CMS access.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Application
Log Sources.Channel: Unexpected web application errors or CMS logs showing modification to index.html, default.aspx, or other public-facing files

Mutable Elements.Description: Environment-specific naming of defacement-prone files like 'index.html', 'main.css', 'app.js'.
Detection based on rapid sequence of file writes and script injections within short time intervals.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Impair Defenses Indicator Blocking
Analysis Description: Correlates registry modifications to EventLog or WMI Autologger keys, suspicious use of Set-EtwTraceProvider, and Sysmon configuration changes. Defender sees interruption or redirection of ETW and log event collection.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of ETW providers to baseline and monitor for unexpected removal.
Whitelist of expected admin actions modifying Sysmon or ETW configurations.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates
Analysis Description: Monitor for abnormal certificate enrollment and usage activity in Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS), registry access to certificate storage locations, and unusual process executions that attempt to export or access private keys.

Mutable Elements.Description: Organizations may tune which Extended Key Usage (EKU) values are considered risky.
Defines how quickly multiple certificate enrollments from the same entity should trigger correlation alerts.
Differentiate between service accounts and interactive user accounts to reduce false positives.

Detection Name: Detect Forged Kerberos Silver Tickets (T1558.002)
Analysis Description: Detects forged Kerberos Silver Tickets by identifying anomalous Kerberos service ticket activity such as malformed fields in logon events, TGS requests without interaction with the KDC, and access attempts using service accounts outside expected hosts/resources. Also monitors suspicious processes accessing LSASS memory for credential dumping.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Kerberos
Log Sources.Channel: Kerberos TGS-REQ anomalies without KDC validation (Silver Ticket behavior)

Mutable Elements.Description: Expected mapping of service accounts to specific resources; deviations may indicate Silver Ticket use.
Expected TGS issuance patterns including KDC validation; anomalies may signal forged tickets.
Known processes that legitimately interact with LSASS; others may indicate dumping attempts.
Correlate Kerberos requests within a tunable timeframe to reduce false positives.

Detection Name: Suspicious Database Access and Dump Activity Across Environments (T1213.006)
Analysis Description: Database client execution (e.g., sqlcmd.exe, isql.exe) by users or from locations not tied to enterprise automation or backups. Often followed by creation of .sql/.bak/.csv files, registry artifacts for ODBC/JDBC drivers, or encrypted ZIPs. Defender sees SQL tools launched by explorer.exe, Powershell, or odd parent processes, plus file writes in user temp locations.

Mutable Elements.Description: Directories where legitimate database tools are installed
List of file extensions commonly used for DB exports
Threshold for outbound data volume that may suggest large DB dumps

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Spearphishing Voice across OS platforms
Analysis Description: Monitor call log records from corporate devices for unusual or unauthorized numbers, especially repeated calls to/from known malicious phone numbers. Correlate with subsequent system events (e.g., browser navigation, remote management tool execution).

Log Sources.Name: ApplicationLog:CallRecords
Log Sources.Channel: Outbound or inbound calls to high-risk or blocklisted numbers

Mutable Elements.Description: List of known malicious or suspicious phone numbers; must be tuned per environment
Threshold for correlating call events with subsequent suspicious system activity

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for MFA Interception via Input Capture and Smart Card Proxying
Analysis Description: Behavior chain involving unexpected API calls to capture keyboard input, driver loads for keyloggers, or remote use of smart card authentication via logon sessions not initiated by local user interaction

Mutable Elements.Description: Tunable based on what memory-level access the keylogger uses (e.g., 0x10 for read)
Legitimate accessibility tools may use similar API calls (e.g., Magnifier.exe)
Define how quickly access + registry mod + smart card use must co-occur

Detection Name: User Execution – Malicious Image (containers & IaaS) – pull/run → start → anomalous behavior (T1204.003)
Analysis Description: IAAS (Cloud images/VMs): A new VM/instance is launched from a non-approved or newly-seen image (AMI/GCP Image/Azure Image). On first boot, cloud-init/user-data or embedded agents download code, spawn system utilities, or open outbound C2/mining traffic. The analytic correlates Instance/Image Creation → Instance Start → in-guest Process/Command Execution and/or anomalous network traffic.

Log Sources.Name: azure:activity
Log Sources.Channel: Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/write: imageReference publisher NOT IN allowlist OR plan is new/unknown

Log Sources.Name: NSM:Flow
Log Sources.Channel: New VM egress to crypto-mining pools or non-approved Internet ranges within minutes of boot

Mutable Elements.Description: Set of golden images/owners and digest/IDs allowed to launch.
Whether to alert when userData/cloud-init contains exec or download directives.
Time after start considered first-boot (e.g., ≤30m) for correlation.
Restrict detection to prod or internet-facing subnets to reduce noise.

Detection Name: Detect Credential Discovery via Windows Registry Enumeration
Analysis Description: Defenders observe command-line executions or API-based registry reads targeting sensitive paths like HKLM or HKCU with keyword filters such as 'password', 'cred', or 'logon'. Typically performed by Reg.exe, PowerShell, custom binaries, or offensive tools such as Cobalt Strike. Correlation with process ancestry and command-line arguments indicates suspicious credential discovery activity.

Log Sources.Name: EDR:hunting
Log Sources.Channel: Behavioral rule for registry enumeration under credential-related paths

Mutable Elements.Description: List of strings searched in registry queries (e.g., password, credential, login). May need to expand for localized OS or app-specific terms.
Parent process used for registry access. Can tune for suspicious ancestry (e.g., cmd.exe > reg.exe vs. services.exe > reg.exe).
Time-based correlation window for detecting chained activity between registry reads and subsequent credential use or exfiltration.
HKLM vs. HKCU vs. others. May limit scope to user or system context depending on risk appetite.

Detection Name: User-Initiated Malicious Library Installation via Package Manager (T1204.005)
Analysis Description: Execution of pip.exe, npm.cmd, or MSI installers within user context, followed by script interpreter startup (e.g., python.exe) or PowerShell with unusual child processes or file writes in %APPDATA%, %TEMP%, or %LOCALAPPDATA%. Defender correlates command-line install tools with Sysmon and Event Logs to trace downstream behavior.

Mutable Elements.Description: Filter expected automation tools (e.g., enterprise installers, known IDEs)
Suspicious post-install write paths (e.g., %APPDATA%, %TEMP%)
Used for evaluating if dropped files are packed/obfuscated

Detection Name: Detection Strategy of Transmitted Data Manipulation
Analysis Description: Monitor for anomalies in transmitted data streams, including mismatched file integrity checks, API interception, or man-in-the-middle modifications. Detect unexpected use of APIs that handle network I/O where transmitted data integrity could be manipulated.

Mutable Elements.Description: Hash baselines or digital signature references to validate transmitted data.
List of ports/services where data integrity validation is enforced.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Log Enumeration
Analysis Description: Monitor for use of native utilities such as wevtutil.exe or PowerShell cmdlets (Get-WinEvent, Get-EventLog) to enumerate or export logs. Unusual access to security or system event channels, especially by non-administrative users or processes, should be correlated with subsequent file export or network transfer activity.

Mutable Elements.Description: Expected log management scripts executed by administrators should be excluded from alerts.
Correlate enumeration attempts with file export or network transfer within a defined timeframe.

Detection Name: Detect Mark-of-the-Web (MOTW) Bypass via Container and Disk Image Files
Analysis Description: Detects extraction or mounting of container/archive files (e.g., .iso, .vhd, .zip) that originated from the Internet but whose contained files lack Zone.Identifier MOTW tagging. Correlates file creation metadata with subsequent execution of unsigned or untrusted binaries launched outside SmartScreen or Protected View.

Mutable Elements.Description: Adjust monitored file types (e.g., .iso, .vhd, .zip, .gz, .rar) based on enterprise usage
Defines correlation window between extraction/mount and first execution of inner files
Whitelist known enterprise archivers and deployment mechanisms to reduce false positives

Detection Name: Remote Desktop Software Execution and Beaconing Detection
Analysis Description: Adversary installation or use of RMM software (e.g., TeamViewer, AnyDesk, ScreenConnect) followed by outbound beaconing or remote session establishment

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-Windows Firewall With Advanced Security/Firewall
Log Sources.Channel: new rule allowing inbound or outbound connections for remote desktop software

Mutable Elements.Description: RMM software can vary; defenders should update rules to account for additional binaries (e.g., ConnectWise, Zoho Assist)
RMM software may use configurable or random high ports outside of standard (e.g., 7070, 5650)
Expected parent process may vary in different enterprise contexts
Correlation window for install-to-beacon or process-to-network event should match operational environment

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Forged Web Credentials
Analysis Description: Forged web credentials on Windows endpoints may be detected by anomalous browser cookie files, local token cache manipulations, or tools injecting tokens into sessions. Defenders may observe processes accessing LSASS or browser credential stores unexpectedly, followed by unusual logon sessions.

Mutable Elements.Description: Define expected processes that access LSASS or browser credential files.

Detection Name: Detection of Local Data Staging Prior to Exfiltration
Analysis Description: Detects file reads across locations followed by writes to temp or staging directories, often compressed or encrypted, indicating local staging behavior.

Mutable Elements.Description: Paths such as C:\Temp, C:\Windows\Tasks, etc.
Matches to 7z.exe, rar.exe, zip.exe, or custom scripts.
How long to correlate file reads followed by compression.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Dynamic Resolution through DNS Calculation
Analysis Description: Monitor DNS query results where subsequent connections use derived or unusual port numbers not explicitly resolved, especially when tied to suspicious processes. Correlate Sysmon DNS logs (Event ID 22) with process creation and socket activity.

Mutable Elements.Description: Deviation from common service ports (e.g., >1024 when DNS resolved service expects 80/443)
Correlation window between DNS response and network connection (e.g., 5 minutes)

Detection Name: Cross-Platform Detection of JavaScript Execution Abuse
Analysis Description: Detects JavaScript execution through WSH (wscript.exe, cscript.exe) or HTA (mshta.exe), particularly when spawned from Office macros, web browsers, or abnormal user paths. Correlates script execution with outbound network activity or system modification.

Mutable Elements.Description: Execution of wscript.exe, cscript.exe, or mshta.exe from suspicious parent like Excel or Outlook.
Script loaded from %TEMP%, user download folder, or via UNC/web path.
Execution of JavaScript during non-business or patch windows.
Execution by accounts not typically authorized for scripting (e.g., non-admin users).
Obfuscated JS with high entropy detected by AMSI or ScriptBlock logging.

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Mailbox Data and Log Deletion for Anti-Forensics
Analysis Description: Detects mailbox manipulation or deletion via PowerShell (e.g., Remove-MailboxExportRequest), file deletion from Outlook data stores (Unistore.db), or tampering with quarantined mail logs.

Mutable Elements.Description: Outlook files in AppData\Local\Comms\Unistore\data
Target suspicious rule changes (e.g., header removal)
Regex match on `Remove-MailboxExportRequest` and similar Exchange cmdlets

Detection Name: Resource Hijacking Detection Strategy
Analysis Description: Persistent high CPU utilization combined with suspicious command-line execution (e.g., mining tools or obfuscated scripts) and outbound connections to mining/proxy networks.

Log Sources.Name: Windows:perfmon
Log Sources.Channel: High sustained CPU usage by a single process

Mutable Elements.Description: Duration threshold for sustained CPU activity (e.g., >15 minutes)
Known mining pool IPs or proxy service endpoints
Regex list of suspicious or known mining tools

Detection Name: Detect Archiving via Library (T1560.002)
Analysis Description: Detects adversarial archiving using libraries (zlib, zip APIs) invoked by scripts or binaries. Correlates process executions of Python, PowerShell, or custom .NET binaries with DLL/module loads linked to compression libraries, followed by archive file creation.

Mutable Elements.Description: Known business applications using compression libraries.
Archive extensions considered sensitive in monitored environments.
Correlation window between script/library invocation and file creation.

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection Strategy for Remote Service Logins and Post-Access Activity
Analysis Description: Logon via RDP or WMI by a user account followed by uncommon command execution, file manipulation, or lateral network connections.

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlation window between remote login and post-access activity
Limit to service accounts or privileged users for higher fidelity
Allowlisting known admin jumpboxes or deployment tools

Detection Name: Detection of Domain or Tenant Policy Modifications via AD and Identity Provider
Analysis Description: Adversary modifies Group Policy Objects (GPOs), domain trust, or directory service objects via GUI, CLI, or programmatic APIs. Behavior includes creation/modification of GPOs, delegation permissions, trust objects, or rogue domain controller registration.

Mutable Elements.Description: Filter to specific AD containers (e.g., CN=Policies,CN=System,DC=domain,DC=com) for GPOs.
Focus on high-risk attributes such as gPCFileSysPath, ntSecurityDescriptor.
Correlate changes with suspicious process creation or privileged user logon.
Alert on unexpected user or service account modifying domain policy.

Detection Name: Detect Domain Controller Authentication Process Modification (Skeleton Key)
Analysis Description: Detects anomalous process access to LSASS on domain controllers, suspicious module loads of authentication DLLs, and registry or file modifications indicative of Skeleton Key–style patching. Correlates LSASS access attempts with subsequent abnormal logon activity patterns.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:System
Log Sources.Channel: Unexpected modification to lsass.exe or cryptdll.dll

Mutable Elements.Description: Specific authentication DLLs such as cryptdll.dll and samsrv.dll monitored for tampering.
Correlation window between LSASS memory access, module load, and suspicious logons.
Baseline expected accounts performing domain controller logon operations.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Encrypted Channel across OS Platforms
Analysis Description: Processes that normally do not initiate network connections establishing outbound encrypted TLS/SSL sessions, especially with asymmetric traffic volumes (client sending more than receiving) or non-standard certificate chains. Defender observations correlate process creation with unexpected network encryption libraries being loaded.

Mutable Elements.Description: Whitelist processes expected to use TLS (e.g., browsers, mail clients).
Payload randomness threshold to distinguish C2 encryption from legitimate traffic.
Correlation window between process creation, module load, and encrypted connection.

Detection Name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution Detection Strategy
Analysis Description: Correlation of registry key modification for Run/RunOnce with abnormal parent-child process relationships and outlier execution at user logon or system startup

Mutable Elements.Description: Customize based on expected parent-child process lineage for autostarts
May vary based on organization policy or installed software

Detection Name: Detect Adversary Deobfuscation or Decoding of Files and Payloads
Analysis Description: An adversary leverages built-in tools such as certutil.exe, powershell.exe, or copy.exe to decode, reassemble, or extract hidden malicious content from obfuscated containers or encoded formats. The decoding utility often spawns shortly after file staging or download and may be chained with script interpreters or further payload execution.

Mutable Elements.Description: May vary across environments (e.g., certutil, powershell, copy, expand, 7zip)
Targets may use .txt, .cer, .enc, .b64, .zip, etc. to disguise payloads
Command syntax varies between base64 decoding, copy /b, and expand switches
Deobfuscation typically follows staging/download within a short timeframe

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Rogue Domain Controller (DCShadow) Registration and Replication Abuse
Analysis Description: Detection of rogue Domain Controller registration and Active Directory replication abuse by correlating: (1) creation/modification of nTDSDSA and server objects in the Configuration partition, (2) unexpected usage of Directory Replication Service SPNs (GC/ or E3514235-4B06-11D1-AB04-00C04FC2DCD2), (3) replication RPC calls (DrsAddEntry, DrsReplicaAdd, GetNCChanges) originating from non-DC hosts, and (4) Kerberos authentication by non-DC machines using DRS-related SPNs. These events in combination, especially from hosts outside the Domain Controllers OU, may indicate DCShadow or rogue DC activity.

Log Sources.Name: m365:dirsync
Log Sources.Channel: Replication cookie changes involving Configuration partition with new server/nTDSDSA objects.

Log Sources.Name: NSM:Flow
Log Sources.Channel: DrsAddEntry, DrsReplicaAdd, GetNCChanges calls between non-DC and DCs.

Mutable Elements.Description: Window (seconds) between nTDSDSA object creation and subsequent replication traffic from same host (default 300s).
List of legitimate DCs authorized for replication to reduce false positives.
SPNs indicating replication service usage (GC/, GUID E3514235-4B06-11D1-AB04-00C04FC2DCD2).
Trigger alerts only when AD object creation is by accounts not in Domain Admins or Enterprise Admins groups.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for T1542 Pre-OS Boot
Analysis Description: Unusual modification of boot records (MBR, VBR) or EFI partitions not associated with legitimate patch cycles or OS upgrades. Registry or WMI events associated with firmware update tools executed from unexpected parent processes. API calls (e.g., DeviceIoControl) writing directly to raw disk sectors. Subsequent abnormal boot configuration changes followed by unsigned driver loads.

Mutable Elements.Description: Legitimate vendor tools or processes authorized to modify firmware or boot records.
Correlating boot-sector modification with subsequent reboot events.
Heuristic threshold for detecting obfuscated/packed boot code.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for System Services across OS platforms.
Analysis Description: Monitor for abnormal creation or modification of Windows services (e.g., via sc.exe, PowerShell, or API calls) that load non-standard executables. Correlate registry changes in service keys with service creation events and process execution to detect service abuse for persistence or execution.

Mutable Elements.Description: Known good services and installers that regularly modify or create services
Threshold for correlating service creation with unusual process execution

Detection Name: Behavior-Based Registry Modification Detection on Windows
Analysis Description: Behavior chain involving abnormal registry modifications via CLI, PowerShell, WMI, or direct API calls, especially targeting persistence, privilege escalation, or defense evasion keys, potentially followed by service restart or process execution. Such as editing Notify/Userinit/Startup keys, or disabling SafeDllSearchMode.

Mutable Elements.Description: Environment-specific list of monitored or critical registry keys, e.g., Run, Services, Security Settings, LSASS
Allowlist of legitimate registry tools (e.g., regedit.exe, msiexec.exe); used to filter known safe writes
Correlate registry change with nearby process/service execution within a defined timeframe
Flag unsigned executables or abnormal parent-child lineage performing registry modification

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Compressed Payload Creation and Execution
Analysis Description: Monitors for compression tool usage (e.g., 7zip, WinRAR, MakeCab) that follows or precedes file modification, suspicious file types (e.g., .exe, .dll) being compressed, or dropped from self-extracting archives followed by immediate execution.

Mutable Elements.Description: Zip, .rar, .cab, .gz – tune based on expected legitimate use of compression in environment
Expected time between archive unpacking and first execution – short delays are suspicious
Restrict detection to non-admin or interactive users if excessive FPs from sys admin activity

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for System Binary Proxy Execution: Regsvr32
Analysis Description: Detection focuses on identifying anomalous regsvr32.exe executions that deviate from normal administrative or system use. Defenders may observe regsvr32.exe loading scriptlets or DLLs from unusual paths (especially temporary directories or remote URLs), command-line arguments invoking /i or /u with suspicious file references, network connections initiated by regsvr32.exe, and unsigned or untrusted DLLs being loaded shortly after regsvr32.exe invocation. Correlated sequences include regsvr32.exe process creation, module load of DLL/scriptlet, and optional outbound network traffic.

Mutable Elements.Description: Directories where DLL loading via regsvr32.exe is expected (e.g., C:\Windows\System32).
File extensions considered suspicious when executed by regsvr32.exe (e.g., .sct, .ocx).
Timeframe to correlate regsvr32.exe process creation with subsequent module loads and network connections.
Parent processes from which regsvr32.exe is expected (e.g., explorer.exe during legitimate COM object registration).

Detection Name: Behavior-chain detection for T1134 Access Token Manipulation on Windows
Analysis Description: Detection of suspicious token manipulation chains: use of token-related APIs (e.g., LogonUser, DuplicateTokenEx) or commands (runas) → spawning of a new process under a different security context (e.g., SYSTEM) → mismatched parent-child process lineage or anomalies in Event Tracing for Windows (ETW) token/PPID data → abnormal lateral or privilege escalation activity.

Log Sources.Name: ETW:Token
Log Sources.Channel: token_analysis: API calls such as DuplicateTokenEx or ImpersonateLoggedOnUser

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlation time between suspicious API usage, runas, and process creation (e.g., 5–10m).
Whitelist of service accounts permitted to spawn SYSTEM-level processes.
Legitimate administrative utilities that trigger token changes.
Deviation threshold for PPID mismatches detected via ETW.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Exfiltration to Text Storage Sites
Analysis Description: Unexpected processes (e.g., powershell.exe, wscript.exe, office apps) initiating HTTP POST/PUT requests to text storage domains like pastebin.com or hastebin.com, particularly when preceded by file access in sensitive directories. Defender perspective: correlation of process lineage, large clipboard/file read operations, and outbound uploads to text storage services.

Mutable Elements.Description: Domains to monitor such as pastebin.com, hastebin.com, ghostbin.com.
Minimum data size (e.g., >500KB) to trigger alerts for suspicious uploads.

Detection Name: Multi-Event Behavioral Detection for DCOM-Based Remote Code Execution
Analysis Description: A remote DCOM invocation by a privileged account using RPC (port 135), followed by abnormal process instantiation or module loading on the remote system indicative of code execution.

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlate RPC activity with remote process creation within a configurable time window (e.g., 300s)
Identify rare or first-time DCOM invocations by specific accounts
List of suspicious executables commonly abused via DCOM (e.g., excel.exe, wmiprvse.exe)
Known set of systems that should or should not be invoking DCOM activity

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Impersonation
Analysis Description: Monitor for anomalous email activity originating from Windows-hosted applications (e.g., Outlook) where the sending account name or display name does not match the underlying SMTP address. Detect abnormal volume of outbound messages containing sensitive keywords (e.g., 'payment', 'wire transfer') or anomalous login locations for accounts associated with email sending activity.

Log Sources.Name: m365:unified
Log Sources.Channel: SendOnBehalf/SendAs: Emails sent where the sending identity mismatches account ownership

Mutable Elements.Description: Adjust impersonation detection keywords based on local business risk terms (e.g., 'ACH', 'Invoice').

Detection Name: Exploitation for Client Execution – cross-platform behavior chain (browser/Office/3rd-party apps)
Analysis Description: Cause→effect chain: (1) A client app (browser, Office, PDF/Flash/reader) experiences a crash/abnormal exit or loads from an unusual location, then (2) drops or modifies a file in user-writable paths, and/or (3) spawns an unexpected child (e.g., powershell/cmd/mshta/rundll32/wscript/installer), and (4) establishes outbound C2-like connections shortly after. Correlate application logs, file writes, process lineage, and network egress within a short window.

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlation window (e.g., 15m) between crash/write/child/network.
List of child processes that should rarely spawn from Office/browsers (powershell.exe, cmd.exe, wscript.exe, mshta.exe, rundll32.exe, regsvr32.exe, msiexec.exe, curl.exe).
Writable paths to watch (Downloads, %TEMP%, %APPDATA%, OneDrive, Office startup folders).
Known add-ins/extensions and updater binaries to reduce noise.
Known update/CDN domains and proxy egress CIDRs for suppression.

Detection Name: Detect Hybrid Identity Authentication Process Modification
Analysis Description: Detects injection or tampering of DLLs in hybrid identity agents (e.g., AzureADConnectAuthenticationAgentService), registry or configuration changes tied to PTA/AD FS, and anomalous LSASS or AD FS module loads correlated with authentication anomalies.

Mutable Elements.Description: Hybrid identity services monitored for tampering, e.g., PTA agent, AD FS.
Window correlating DLL/module load events with logon anomalies.

Detection Name: User Execution – Malicious File via download/open → spawn chain (T1204.002)
Analysis Description: User opens a file delivered by email, web, chat, or share. The handler application (Word/PDF reader/archiver) creates a file in user-controlled paths (Downloads, Temp, Desktop) and then spawns a new or unusual child process (e.g., powershell.exe, wscript.exe, cmd.exe, regsvr32.exe, rundll32.exe, msiexec.exe). Optional precursors include FileStreamCreated (URL/UNC) and Office → system32 batch writes.

Mutable Elements.Description: Seconds/minutes to correlate file write to child spawn (e.g., 0–5m).
Extensions and double-extension patterns to flag (exe,scr,lnk,pif,cpl,js,vbs,bat,cmd,ps1,hta,iso,lnk->cmd,docm,xlsm,pdf->exe, etc.).
Paths considered user-controlled (Downloads, Temp, Desktop, profile AppData staging).
List of user-facing apps that commonly open attachments for your org (reduce FPs or add weight).
Trusted code signers/publishers to suppress benign admin tools.

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Thread Execution Hijacking via Thread Suspension and Context Switching
Analysis Description: Detects hijacking of an existing thread (OpenThread) through a behavioral chain involving thread suspension (SuspendThread), memory modification (VirtualAllocEx + WriteProcessMemory), context manipulation (SetThreadContext), and thread resumption—all within another live process's address space (ResumeThread).

Mutable Elements.Description: Sensitive processes that should never be targeted for thread hijack attempts
Expected delay between SuspendThread and ResumeThread events; tight thresholds reduce evasion
Memory regions or offsets that should not be targeted for SetThreadContext
Score deviation of the parent/child relationship in a thread injection chain

Detection Name: Detect Adversary-in-the-Middle via Network and Configuration Anomalies
Analysis Description: Detects suspicious DNS/ARP poisoning attempts, unauthorized modifications to registry/network configuration, or abnormal TLS downgrade activity. Correlates changes in system configuration with subsequent unusual network flows or authentication events.

Mutable Elements.Description: Specific network stack and DNS registry keys that vary by enterprise configuration.
List of weak/legacy ciphers tuned per environment for TLS downgrade detection.
Correlation period between config changes and abnormal network connections.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Disk Structure Wipe via Boot/Partition Overwrite
Analysis Description: Processes attempting raw disk access to overwrite sensitive structures such as the MBR or partition table using \.\PhysicalDrive notation. Detection relies on correlating process creation, privilege escalation, and raw sector writes in Sysmon and Security logs.

Log Sources.Channel: Raw write attempts targeting \\.\PhysicalDrive0 or sector 0 (MBR/partition table)

Detection Name: Detect Archiving via Utility (T1560.001)
Analysis Description: Detects adversarial archiving using built-in or third-party utilities (makecab, diantz, xcopy, certutil, 7z, WinRAR, WinZip). Correlates suspicious process creation events with command-line arguments for compression/encoding, followed by creation of archive files (.cab, .zip, .7z, .rar). Identifies anomalous loading of crypt32.dll for encryption operations or execution of diantz.exe to compress remotely staged files.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of archive extensions considered high risk (.cab, .zip, .7z, .rar).

Minimum archive size threshold to filter out benign small compressions.

Detection Name: Multi-Platform File and Directory Permissions Modification Detection Strategy
Analysis Description: Sequential behavioral chain of privilege escalation through permission modification: (1) Process creation of permission-modifying utilities (icacls, takeown, attrib, cacls), (2) Correlation with unusual user context or timing, (3) DACL modification events targeting sensitive files/directories, (4) Subsequent file access or modification attempts indicating successful privilege bypass

Mutable Elements.Description: Temporal correlation window for linking permission modification with subsequent access attempts (default: 300 seconds)
Environment-specific critical file and directory paths requiring permission change monitoring
Administrative accounts authorized to perform legitimate permission modifications
Time-based threshold for elevated alerting on permission changes outside business hours

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Reflective Code Loading
Analysis Description: Detect anomalous chains of memory allocation and execution inside the same process (e.g., VirtualAlloc → memcpy → VirtualProtect → CreateThread). Unlike process injection, reflective code loading does not perform cross-process memory writes — the suspicious activity occurs entirely within the process’s own PID context.

Detection Name: Removable Media Execution Chain Detection via File and Process Activity
Analysis Description: Execution of files originating from removable media after drive mount, with correlation to file write activity, autorun usage, or lateral spread via staged tools.

Detection Name: Port-knock → rule/daemon change → first successful connect (T1205.001)
Analysis Description: A remote source rapidly touches a short sequence of closed ports (SYN→RST/S0) on a Windows host. Detection Name: Local Account Enumeration Across Host Platforms

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Endpoint DoS via Application or System Exploitation
Analysis Description: Exploitation of system or application vulnerability (e.g., CVE-based exploit) followed by service crash, restart, or repeated failure within a short time frame, impacting application/system availability.

Detection Name: Detection of Group Policy Modifications via AD Object Changes and File Activity

Alert on unexpected modification by non-admins or uncommon accounts.

Detection Name: Detect Access to Unsecured Credential Files Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Correlated file access to insecure credential files (e.g., .env, .xml, *.ps1) followed by suspicious process execution or authentication using retrieved credentials. Detected through Sysmon logs and Windows Security Event logs.

Detection Name: Compromised software/update chain (installer/write → first-run/child → egress/signature anomaly)

Allow-list of vendor update endpoints, enterprise proxy/cache.
Code-signing publishers allowed for programs/services.

Detection Name: Suspicious Addition to Local or Domain Groups
Analysis Description: Detects unauthorized additions of users or machine accounts to privileged local or domain groups (e.g., Administrators, Remote Desktop Users).

Detection Name: Detection for Spoofing Security Alerting across OS Platforms
Analysis Description: Detection of inconsistencies between reported sensor health and actual process/service state. Detection Name: Detect Active Setup Persistence via StubPath Execution
Analysis Description: Multi-event correlation of Registry creation under Active Setup with anomalous execution of processes at user logon. Behavioral patterns include creation/modification of HKLM Active Setup keys with non-standard StubPath values, followed by process execution from uncommon paths, unsigned binaries, or unusual parent-child lineage post-user login.

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlate registry change and process execution within a specific user logon session (e.g., 5–10 minutes)

Degree of randomness/uncommonness in StubPath values. High entropy may indicate obfuscation.

Check which user/context added the Active Setup key to detect privilege abuse

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for HTML Smuggling via JavaScript Blob + Dynamic File Drop
Analysis Description: Detection of browser-based or email client-driven file creation (often from temp directories) following navigation to or execution of HTML files containing JavaScript Blob APIs or base64 Data URLs, with follow-on execution of the dropped payload. Analysis Description: Adversary gains high integrity or special privileges (e.g., SeDebugPrivilege), locates a running browser process, opens it with write/inject rights, and modifies it (e.g., CreateRemoteThread / DLL load) to inherit cookies/tokens or establish a browser pivot.
