Mutable Elements.Description: Set of monitored browsers (chrome.exe, msedge.exe, firefox.exe, iexplore.exe). Adjust per fleet.
Access rights implying injection (e.g., 0x1FFFFF, 0x1F3FF, VM_WRITE, VM_OPERATION, CREATE_THREAD). Tune by EDR mapping.
Allowed module signers within browser processes (e.g., Microsoft, Google). Helps flag unsigned/unknown ImageLoad into browsers.
Enterprise internal ranges or DNS suffixes to identify intranet pivoting via the browser.
Correlation interval (e.g., 10–20 minutes) linking privilege gain → access → modification → network usage.
Legitimate tools that may automate browsers (e.g., Selenium drivers). Reduce FPs by allowlisting.
Scope analytics to high-value users, admin workstations, or servers where browsers shouldn’t be automated.

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Process Injection Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Detects process injection by correlating memory manipulation API calls (e.g., VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory), suspicious thread creation (e.g., CreateRemoteThread), and unusual DLL loads within another process's context.

Mutable Elements.Description: Specific access rights used during process handle acquisition, e.g., PROCESS_VM_WRITE
Time correlation window between API calls and thread creation events
Known high-value targets often abused for injection (e.g., lsass.exe, explorer.exe)

Detection Name: Detection of Web Session Cookie Theft via File, Memory, and Network Artifacts
Analysis Description: Detects suspicious access to browser session cookie storage (e.g., Chrome’s Cookies SQLite DB) or memory reads of browser processes. Anomalous injection or memory dump utilities targeting browser processes such as chrome.exe, firefox.exe, or msedge.exe.

Mutable Elements.Description: Monitored browsers (e.g., chrome.exe, firefox.exe)
Suspicious tools used for injection or memory access (e.g., mimikatz, procdump)
Locations of cookie stores like `AppData\Local\Google\Chrome\User Data\Default\Cookies`

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for SVG Smuggling with Script Execution and Delivery Behavior
Analysis Description: Detects suspicious SVG file creation or download events followed by script engine execution (e.g., wscript.exe, mshta.exe, rundll32.exe), network callbacks, or browser-based credential collection.

Mutable Elements.Description: Threshold between SVG file write and script execution (e.g., < 60s)
Allowlisted script engines that may invoke browsers or JS in benign cases
Regex or string match for .svg, .svgz, or embedded .svg inside HTML or PDF

Detection Name: Detection of Data Access and Collection from Removable Media
Analysis Description: Adversary mounts a USB device and begins enumerating, copying, or compressing files using scripting engines, cmd, or remote access tools.

Mutable Elements.Description: Can tune based on known removable device labels or whitelist
Controls timing between device mount and sensitive file access
Tune for sensitive file extensions (e.g., .docx, .pdf, .csv)

Detection Name: Detection of Exfiltration Over Asymmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol
Technique Name: Exfiltration Over Asymmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol
Analysis Description: Detects non-browser processes that establish encrypted outbound connections (e.g., TLS/SSL) to unfamiliar or atypical destinations for the host/user, following a data staging or compression event.

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlates file access, encryption, and network transmission within a timeframe (e.g., 5 minutes).
Blocks or flags untrusted certificate authorities in SSL/TLS handshakes.
Whitelist for known-good applications allowed to use encrypted outbound traffic.

Detection Name: Detection of Cached Domain Credential Dumping via Local Hash Cache Access
Analysis Description: Detects adversary behavior accessing Windows cached domain credential files using tools like Mimikatz, reg.exe, or PowerShell, often combined with registry exports or LSASS memory scraping.

Mutable Elements.Description: Location of cached credential files may vary with OS version or custom registry hive exports.
Patterns for reg save, secretsdump, or PowerShell dumping tools may be tuned to org-specific tooling.
Temporal correlation window between process execution and registry/file access.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Exploitation for Privilege Escalation
Analysis Description: Detects exploitation attempts targeting vulnerable kernel drivers or OS components, often followed by unusual process or token behavior.

Mutable Elements.Description: Targeted BYOVD drivers may vary based on campaign and tooling.
Controls temporal linking of driver load → process spawn → privilege use.
Parent-child relationships vary by exploitation vector (e.g., LOLBin vs. dropper).

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Command and Scripting Interpreter Abuse
Analysis Description: Detects the execution of scripting or command interpreters (e.g., powershell.exe, cmd.exe, wscript.exe) outside expected administrative time windows or from abnormal user contexts, often followed by encoded/obfuscated arguments or secondary execution events.

Mutable Elements.Description: Tunable to match encoded or uncommon script execution patterns specific to the environment.
May vary across managed/unmanaged workstations or user-driven script activity.
Used to restrict analysis to work hours or known admin maintenance windows.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Hijack Execution Flow through the AppDomainManager on Windows.
Analysis Description: Detection focuses on unauthorized manipulation of .NET AppDomainManager behavior. Defenders may observe suspicious creation of new AppDomains within trusted processes, anomalous loading of assemblies via non-standard configuration files, or registry/environment variable changes redirecting AppDomainManager to malicious assemblies. Correlated events include config file tampering, new process creation of .NET host processes (e.g., w3wp.exe, powershell.exe) with modified runtime parameters, and module loads of unusual or unsigned .NET DLLs.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of monitored .NET host processes (e.g., powershell.exe, w3wp.exe, svchost.exe).
Known benign .NET assemblies expected to load via AppDomainManager.
Directory paths where configuration tampering should be monitored (application directories, system32, program files).
Correlation period between file modification of config/environment settings and subsequent anomalous module load.

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of T1498 – Network Denial of Service Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Executable or script generating large outbound network traffic targeting remote hosts or known amplification ports

Mutable Elements.Description: Number of connections per second that should trigger anomaly logic
Count of unique destination IPs or ports

Detection Name: Detect Persistence via Office Template Macro Injection or Registry Hijack
Analysis Description: Adversaries inject VBA macros into Office templates such as Normal.dotm or Personal.xlsb or redirect Office template load path via registry key (GlobalDotName) to gain persistence. Template macros trigger execution of malicious code on application startup.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Microsoft-Office-Alerts
Log Sources.Channel: Office application warning or alert on macro execution from template

Mutable Elements.Description: Path to Normal.dotm, Personal.xlsb, or Excel/Word startup templates may vary by Office version and user
GlobalDotName or equivalent registry keys may differ across Office versions or deployments
Office process creation and macro execution timing after system or user login
May be scoped to high-value users or those with access to sensitive templates

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Spoofed GUI Credential Prompts
Analysis Description: Detects suspicious use of PowerShell, .NET, or script interpreters to spawn processes that mimic UAC prompts, often with credential capture dialogue boxes invoked from non-standard parent processes.

Mutable Elements.Description: Tunable to detect suspicious prompts like 'Enter your password' or 'CredentialRequired'
Tune to flag UI prompts spawned from unexpected processes like cmd.exe or user scripts
Scope correlation of script execution and prompt appearance

Detection Name: Detect Kerberos Ticket Theft or Forgery (T1558)
Analysis Description: Detects anomalous Kerberos activity such as forged or stolen tickets by correlating malformed fields in logon events, RC4-encrypted TGTs, or TGS requests without corresponding TGT requests. Also detects suspicious processes accessing LSASS memory for ticket extraction.

Mutable Elements.Description: Threshold for Kerberos TGT lifetimes deviating from domain defaults.
Monitor for downgraded encryption types (e.g., RC4) in Kerberos tickets.
List of expected processes accessing LSASS; deviations may be suspicious.

Detection Name: Detect Code Signing Policy Modification (Windows & macOS)
Analysis Description: Monitors execution of administrative utilities (e.g., bcdedit.exe) or registry modifications that disable Driver Signature Enforcement (DSE) or enable Test Signing. Correlates command-line activity, registry changes, and subsequent process executions that bypass signing enforcement.

Mutable Elements.Description: Expand or restrict monitored utilities (e.g., bcdedit.exe, reg.exe) based on enterprise usage
Customize registry paths tied to Driver Signing enforcement depending on OS version
Correlation window between registry modification and subsequent unsigned binary execution

Detection Name: Traffic Signaling (Port-knock / magic-packet → firewall or service activation) – T1205
Analysis Description: A remote host sends a short sequence of failed connection attempts (RST/ICMP unreachable) to a set of closed ports. Within a brief window the endpoint (a) adds/enables a firewall rule or (b) a sniffer-backed process begins listening or opens a new socket, after which a successful connection occurs. Also detects Wake-on-LAN magic packets seen on local segment.

Mutable Elements.Description: Window to correlate knock sequence → rule change → successful connect (e.g., 120s).
Minimum number of distinct closed ports hit before success (e.g., 3).
List of binaries that commonly toggle firewall/sniff (netsh.exe, powershell.exe, npcapservice.exe, windivert, rawsock tools).
Service accounts or software update agents allowed to change firewall.
Maintenance windows when magic packets are expected.

Detection Name: System Discovery via Native and Remote Utilities
Analysis Description: Process creation and command-line execution of native system discovery utilities such as systeminfo, hostname, wmic, or use of PowerShell/WMI for system enumeration.

Mutable Elements.Description: Detect multiple discovery commands executed in short succession.
Scope alerts to unusual user accounts or service accounts.

Detection Name: Detect Archiving and Encryption of Collected Data (T1560)
Analysis Description: Detects adversarial archiving of files prior to exfiltration by correlating execution of compression/encryption utilities (e.g., makecab.exe, rar.exe, 7z.exe, powershell Compress-Archive) with subsequent creation of large compressed or encrypted files. Identifies abnormal process lineage involving crypt32.dll usage, command-line arguments invoking compression switches, and file write operations to temporary or staging directories.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of file extensions treated as suspicious when created outside of expected paths.
Known business processes permitted to use compression/encryption utilities.
Minimum file size for flagging archive creation to reduce noise from benign small compressions.

Detection Name: Right-to-Left Override Masquerading Detection via Filename and Execution Context
Analysis Description: Execution of files containing right-to-left override characters (U+202E) to masquerade true file extensions. Often found in phishing payloads or file downloads.

Mutable Elements.Description: RTLO variants such as \u202E, %E2%80%AE, or byte-encoded forms
Allows tuning for untrusted sources, e.g., browser downloads or email attachments
Defines correlation between file creation and process execution

Detection Name: Detecting Remote Script Proxy Execution via PubPrn.vbs
Analysis Description: Execution of PubPrn.vbs via cscript.exe using the 'script:' moniker to load and execute a remote .sct scriptlet file, bypassing signature validation and proxying remote payloads through a signed Microsoft script host.

Mutable Elements.Description: Detects 'script:' moniker with HTTP/HTTPS URI as argument to pubprn.vbs
May vary between cscript.exe, wscript.exe, or cmd.exe depending on execution method
Used to detect external domains being contacted for remote scriptlet execution
Maximum allowed time delta between pubprn.vbs invocation and network connection or child process

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of Native API Invocation via Unusual DLL Loads and Direct Syscalls
Analysis Description: Unusual or suspicious processes loading critical native API DLLs (e.g., ntdll.dll, kernel32.dll) followed by direct syscall behavior, memory manipulation, or hollowing.

Mutable Elements.Description: May tune DLL filters to focus on low-level API providers (e.g., ntdll.dll)
Tune for expected parent processes (e.g., explorer.exe, winlogon.exe)
Scope to suspicious targets like LSASS, csrss, etc.

Detection Name: Multi-Event Detection for SMB Admin Share Lateral Movement
Analysis Description: An SMB-based remote file share access followed by lateral movement actions such as remote service creation, task scheduling, or suspicious process execution on the target host using ADMIN$ or C$ shares.

Mutable Elements.Description: Targeted admin share path, such as C$, ADMIN$, IPC$
Correlation window between remote file access and remote execution (e.g., 5-10 minutes)
Distinguish expected remote administrators vs. rare/first-time access by specific users
List of suspicious binaries or tools executed post remote copy (e.g., cmd.exe, powershell.exe, runonce.exe)

Detection Name: Detection of Event Log Clearing on Windows via Behavioral Chain
Analysis Description: Detects behavioral sequence where an adversary gains elevated privileges and clears event logs using native binaries (e.g., wevtutil), PowerShell, or direct file deletion of .evtx files.

Mutable Elements.Description: Time range between log-clearing command and 1102 event; tunable to reduce false positives
Filter by admin/elevated users; allow tuning to detect abuse of high-privilege accounts
Match common variations of log-clearing commands like `Remove-EventLog`, `wevtutil cl`
Scope detection to Security, System, Application, or custom logs based on environment

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Wi-Fi Networks
Analysis Description: Detects anomalous wireless connections such as unexpected SSID associations, failed or repeated authentication attempts, and connections outside of known geofenced networks. Defenders should monitor wireless connection logs and event codes for network discovery, authentication, and association events.

Mutable Elements.Description: Defines approved Wi-Fi SSIDs for the environment; deviations may indicate malicious connection attempts.
Correlates expected physical location of systems with observed Wi-Fi connections to detect anomalies.

Detection Name: Behavioral detection for Supply Chain Compromise (package/update tamper → install → first-run)
Analysis Description: 1) New or updated software is delivered/installed from atypical sources or with signature/hash mismatches; 2) installer/updater writes binaries to unexpected paths or replaces existing signed files; 3) first run causes unsigned/abnormally signed modules to load or child processes to execute, optionally followed by network egress to new destinations.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-CodeIntegrity/Operational
Log Sources.Channel: CodeIntegrity reports 'Invalid image hash' or 'Unsigned image' for new/updated binaries

Log Sources.Name: NSM:Flow
Log Sources.Channel: First-time egress from host after new install to unknown update endpoints

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlation window between install events and first-run activity (default 2h; adjust for staged rollouts).
Publisher/Signer allow-list to suppress expected updates.
Known update CDNs/APIs (e.g., download.microsoft.com) to reduce egress false positives.
Score cut-off for alerting when combining path, signer, and reputation features.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Protocol Tunneling accross OS platforms.
Analysis Description: Processes such as plink.exe, ssh.exe, or netsh.exe establishing outbound network connections where traffic patterns show encapsulated protocols (e.g., RDP over SSH). Defender observations include anomalous process-to-network relationships, large asymmetric data flows, and port usage mismatches.

Mutable Elements.Description: Whitelist legitimate tunneling tools (e.g., used by admins).
Ratio of sent vs received bytes that indicates tunneling activity.
Correlate process creation with network connection within N seconds.

Detection Name: Multi-Platform Behavioral Detection for Compute Hijacking
Analysis Description: Sustained execution of resource-intensive processes (e.g., cryptocurrency miners), often launched via scheduled tasks, WMI, or PowerShell. These processes frequently establish persistent external connections and attempt to evade detection using masqueraded or renamed binaries.

Mutable Elements.Description: The executable name of the miner or wrapper—can vary across campaigns.
May differ depending on the mining pool or proxy server.
Useful for filtering known-good automation vs malicious task runners.

Detection Name: Registry and LSASS Monitoring for Security Support Provider Abuse
Analysis Description: Monitor registry modifications to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\Security Packages or ...\OSConfig\Security Packages, especially insertions of new DLL entries. Correlate this with subsequent DLL module loads into lsass.exe. Track unsigned or anomalous DLLs loading into LSASS using image load auditing. LSASS loads unsigned DLL due to AuditLevel=8 registry configuration or System reboot followed by DLL load into lsass.exe

Mutable Elements.Description: Controls how long after registry modification to expect a DLL load into LSASS (e.g., after reboot)
Use to detect unsigned DLLs or those not matching known trusted publisher certificates
Define allowed SSP values for your org to reduce false positives
Whether detection should correlate boot-time registry and process events

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Encrypted Channel via Asymmetric Cryptography across OS Platforms
Analysis Description: Processes not typically associated with encryption loading asymmetric crypto libraries (e.g., rsaenh.dll, crypt32.dll) and subsequently initiating outbound TLS/SSL connections with abnormal certificate chains or handshakes. Defender correlates process creation, module load, and unusual encrypted sessions.

Mutable Elements.Description: Whitelist browsers, mail clients, or apps expected to use asymmetric crypto.
Baseline CA list for validating abnormal certs.
Detection of incomplete or malformed handshakes.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Process Doppelgänging on Windows
Analysis Description: Detects adversary abuse of Transactional NTFS (TxF) and undocumented process loading mechanisms (e.g., NtCreateProcessEx) to create a hollowed process from an uncommitted, maliciously tainted file image in memory, later executed via NtCreateThreadEx.

Mutable Elements.Description: Pattern of legitimate executables often used as doppelgänging targets (e.g., svchost.exe, calc.exe)
Time delta between TxF rollback and thread creation in hollowed process
Entropy level of thread start address in memory used to detect obfuscated shellcode
Limit on CreateTransaction + RollbackTransaction sequences per process

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for T1505 - Server Software Component
Analysis Description: Installation of malicious IIS/Apache/SQL server modules that later execute command-line interpreters or establish outbound connections.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Application
Log Sources.Channel: Unusual DLL/plugin registration for IIS/SQL/Apache or unexpected error logs

Mutable Elements.Description: Time delta between module install and process execution (e.g., persistence delay).
Custom server wrapper processes or renamed webserver processes may require tuning.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Exfiltration Over Web Service
Analysis Description: Processes that normally do not initiate network communications suddenly making outbound HTTPS connections with high outbound-to-inbound data ratios. Defender view: correlation between process creation logs (e.g., Word, Excel, PowerShell) and subsequent anomalous network traffic volumes toward common web services (Dropbox, Google Drive, OneDrive).

Mutable Elements.Description: List of legitimate web services to baseline (Dropbox, OneDrive, Google Drive).
Outbound data threshold for flagging unusual activity, tunable by environment.
Aggregation period to calculate anomalies in outbound data volume.

Detection Name: Detect Suspicious Access to Private Key Files and Export Attempts Across Platforms
Analysis Description: A process (non-system or user-initiated) accesses private key files in user profile paths or system certificate stores followed by potential network connections or compression activity.

Mutable Elements.Description: Regex for matching key file extensions (.pem, .pfx, .ppk, etc.) or known certificate directories like C:\Users\*\.ssh\
Set of known benign certificate management tools to exclude (e.g., certutil.exe, ssh.exe)

Detection Name: Password Guessing via Multi-Source Authentication Failure Correlation
Analysis Description: Series of authentication failures (Event ID 4625) targeting the same or similar user accounts over time from one or more remote IPs

Mutable Elements.Description: Defines the period in which multiple failed attempts are aggregated (e.g., 10 minutes)
Filter for common account naming conventions, e.g., service accounts or administrator variants
Limit on unique IPs trying to authenticate against a single account

Detection Name: Detection of Windows Service Creation or Modification
Analysis Description: Detects creation or modification of Windows Services through command-line tools (e.g., sc.exe, powershell.exe), Registry key changes under HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services, and service execution under SYSTEM with unsigned or anomalous binary paths. Detects privilege escalation via driver installation or CreateServiceW usage. Correlates parent-child lineage, startup behavior, and rare service names.

Mutable Elements.Description: Regex for suspicious or uncommon service names (e.g., `svhostx`, `winhelp`, etc.)
Flag services whose image path resides in uncommon directories (e.g., `C:\Users\`, `C:\Temp\`)
Watch for `.sys` files loaded by `sc`, Registry, or `ZwLoadDriver` APIs
Temporal window to correlate Registry `Start` key changes with service creation
Raise alerts for unsigned binaries registered as services

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Obfuscated Files or Information: Binary Padding
Analysis Description: Detects the creation or execution of padded binary files (e.g., large size but minimal legitimate content) followed by process execution or lateral movement from the host.

Mutable Elements.Description: Threshold size in MB to determine suspicious padding
Correlation time window between file creation and execution
Scope the detection to suspicious or non-standard user accounts

Detection Name: Detection of Bluetooth-Based Data Exfiltration
Analysis Description: Detection of non-interactive or suspicious processes accessing Bluetooth interfaces and transmitting outbound traffic following file access or staging activity.

Mutable Elements.Description: Defines how quickly a file access and Bluetooth activity must occur to be correlated.
May focus on Bluetooth-specific interfaces or drivers like 'bthport.sys'.
Tune to trigger only on significant exfiltratable file reads.

Detection Name: Behavior-chain detection strategy for T1127.001 Trusted Developer Utilities Proxy Execution: MSBuild (Windows)
Analysis Description: MSBuild.exe is invoked outside expected developer/build contexts or with anomalous arguments (e.g., non-canonical paths, remote shares, Base64/obfuscated property values). Within a short window, it (a) spawns high-risk LOLBins/script interpreters, (b) writes new PE/DLL/script artifacts into user-writable paths and executes them, (c) loads unsigned/user-writable modules, (d) performs memory injection/thread creation into other processes, and/or (e) initiates outbound network connections.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-CodeIntegrity/Operational
Log Sources.Channel: Unsigned/invalid signature modules or images loaded by msbuild.exe or its children

Log Sources.Name: EDR:AMSI
Log Sources.Channel: Malicious inline C#/script blobs embedded in MSBuild projects if intercepted by AMSI-aware loaders (rare but possible via chained LOLBins)

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlation window between msbuild.exe start, payload write, suspicious child spawn, and network (e.g., 0–30 minutes).
Tag/allowlist known developer or CI/CD hosts to reduce noise.
High-risk children (powershell.exe, rundll32.exe, regsvr32.exe, cmd.exe, wscript.exe, mshta.exe) spawned by msbuild.exe.
Regex of user-writable or atypical paths (e.g., %TEMP%, %APPDATA%, OneDrive sync dirs) used to drop payloads.
Tighten alerting to cases with invalid or missing signatures on modules/children.
Minimum rarity/risk score for external destinations to alert.
Numeric threshold for fused, scored correlation (e.g., ≥70/100 triggers an alert).

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Event Triggered Execution: AppInit DLLs (Windows)
Analysis Description: Registry key modification to AppInit_DLLs value followed by anomalous DLL loading by processes importing user32.dll, especially unsigned or uncommon DLLs, suggesting unauthorized AppInit persistence or privilege escalation.

Mutable Elements.Description: Paths or filenames of known-good DLLs to exclude from alerting
Context of the user modifying the registry key (e.g., admin vs standard user)
Temporal threshold for correlating registry modification and DLL load
Filter or flag unsigned or suspiciously signed DLLs

Detection Name: Multi-Platform Shutdown or Reboot Detection via Execution and Host Status Events
Analysis Description: Correlate process execution of shutdown/reboot commands (e.g., shutdown.exe, restart-computer) with host status change logs (Event IDs 1074, 6006) and absence of related administrative context (e.g., user not in Helpdesk group).

Mutable Elements.Description: Defines if user has appropriate privileges to initiate shutdown/reboot.
Unexpected shutdowns during business hours may warrant increased scrutiny.

Detection Name: Detection of Valid Account Abuse Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Detection of compromised or misused valid accounts via anomalous logon patterns, abnormal logon types, and inconsistent geographic or time-based activity across Windows endpoints.

Mutable Elements.Description: Flag unexpected logon types (e.g., Type 10 for remote interactive logins) for sensitive accounts.
Define acceptable hours for interactive logon activity (e.g., 9AM-6PM local).
Trigger on location anomalies based on prior user behavior or policy.

Detection Name: Detect malicious IDE extension install/usage and IDE tunneling
Analysis Description: Adversary installs or side-loads an IDE extension (VS Code, IntelliJ/JetBrains, Eclipse) or enables IDE tunneling. Chain: (1) IDE binary starts on a non-developer endpoint or server, often with install/force/tunnel flags → (2) extension files/registrations appear under user profile → (3) browser/IDE initiates outbound connections to extension marketplaces, update endpoints, or IDE remote/tunnel services → (4) optional child tools (ssh, node, powershell) execute under the IDE context.

Mutable Elements.Description: Executable names/paths (e.g., code.exe, idea64.exe, eclipse.exe, jetbrains-gateway.exe) vary by version and packaging.
Flags such as --install-extension, --force, --disable-extensions, --user-data-dir, --uninstall-extension, tunnel/remote flags are tunable.
List of hosts where IDEs should never run (prod servers, DCs).
Approved extension marketplaces/ide services; use to suppress benign traffic.
Correlation horizon (e.g., 15–30m) between process start, file writes, and outbound IDE/tunnel connections.

Detection Name: Multi-Platform Execution Guardrails Environmental Validation Detection Strategy
Analysis Description: Windows environmental validation behavioral chain: (1) Rapid system discovery reconnaissance through WMI queries, registry enumeration, and network share discovery, (2) Environment-specific artifact collection (hostname, domain, IP addresses, installed software, hardware identifiers), (3) Cryptographic operations or conditional logic based on collected environmental values, (4) Selective payload execution contingent on environmental validation results, (5) Temporal correlation between discovery activities and subsequent execution or network communication

Mutable Elements.Description: Maximum time window for correlating multiple discovery activities indicating reconnaissance phase - adjust based on normal system behavior (default: 300 seconds)
Minimum number of different discovery techniques within time window to trigger detection - balance between false positives and coverage (default: 4 activities)
Approved cryptographic libraries and modules for legitimate organizational use - maintain based on approved software inventory
Complexity score for WMI queries indicating reconnaissance vs. legitimate administration - tune based on administrative patterns
Environment-specific values commonly targeted by guardrails (hostnames, domains, network shares) - customize for organizational environment
Statistical baseline for normal delay between discovery and execution activities - establish through historical analysis

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Impair Defenses via Impair Command History Logging across OS platforms.
Analysis Description: Detection of PowerShell history suppression using Set-PSReadLineOption with SaveNothing or altered HistorySavePath. Correlating these options with PowerShell usage highlights adversarial evasion attempts.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of acceptable PowerShell history save paths for baseline comparison.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Hijack Execution Flow using Path Interception by Search Order Hijacking
Technique Name: Path Interception by Search Order Hijacking
Analysis Description: Processes executing binaries named after legitimate system utilities (e.g., net.exe, findstr.exe, python.exe) from non-standard or application-specific directories, combined with file creation or modification events for such binaries. Defender correlates file writes in vulnerable directories, process execution paths inconsistent with baseline system paths, and abnormal parent-child relationships in process lineage.

Mutable Elements.Description: Common system utilities often hijacked (e.g., net.exe, cmd.exe, powershell.exe, python.exe).
Directories where executables should not normally be written (e.g., application folders, user profile subdirs).
Correlation window between file creation and subsequent process execution.
Expected parent processes for critical system binaries, deviations may indicate hijacking.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for System Language Discovery
Analysis Description: Registry access to system language keys (e.g., HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Nls\Language) or suspicious processes invoking locale-related APIs (e.g., GetUserDefaultUILanguage, GetSystemDefaultUILanguage, GetKeyboardLayoutList). Defender visibility focuses on anomalous or non-standard processes issuing these queries, especially when run by unknown binaries or scripts.

Mutable Elements.Description: Defines trusted processes allowed to query registry language keys or APIs. Unexpected parent-child process chains may indicate adversary use.
Frequency threshold for language registry or API calls within a set time window.

Detection Name: Template Injection Detection - Windows
Analysis Description: Detection of Office or document viewer processes (e.g., winword.exe) initiating network connections to remote templates or executing scripts due to manipulated template references (e.g., embedded in .docx, .rtf, or .dotm files), followed by suspicious child process creation (e.g., PowerShell).

Mutable Elements.Description: Can be tuned to flag known bad domains or external resources in template fields.
May be environment-specific; typically Word, Excel, PowerPoint.
Correlation window for process + network activity.
Trigger when document-spawned child process deviates from expected profile.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Input Injection
Analysis Description: Detects suspicious USB HID device enumeration and keystroke injection patterns, such as rapid sequences of input with no user context, scripts executed through simulated keystrokes, or rogue devices presenting themselves as keyboards.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of known, legitimate USB vendor/product IDs authorized for use in the enterprise.
Restrict detection to times when no user is logged in or activity is outside business hours.
List of legitimate parent processes expected to spawn PowerShell or scripting engines.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Exfiltration to Cloud Storage
Analysis Description: Unusual processes (e.g., powershell.exe, excel.exe) accessing large local files and subsequently initiating HTTPS POST requests to domains associated with cloud storage services (e.g., dropbox.com, drive.google.com, box.com). Defender perspective: correlation between file reads in sensitive directories and high outbound traffic volume to known storage APIs.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of monitored domains for cloud services (dropbox.com, drive.google.com, onedrive.live.com).
Data volume threshold (e.g., >10MB in single session) used to flag abnormal transfers.
User accounts permitted to use sanctioned cloud services versus unexpected accounts.

Detection Name: Detection of System Process Creation or Modification Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Detects command-line or API-based creation/modification of Windows Services via sc.exe, powershell.exe, services.exe, or ChangeServiceConfig. Looks for creation/modification of autostart services via registry changes, file drops to System32\services, and anomalous parent-child process trees.

Mutable Elements.Description: Regex patterns to flag unusual service names or binaries
List of non-administrative processes starting service management tools
Monitored autorun locations (e.g., `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services`)

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Remote System Enumeration Behavior
Analysis Description: Execution of network enumeration utilities (e.g., net.exe, ping.exe, tracert.exe) in short succession, often chained with lateral movement tools or system enumeration commands.

Mutable Elements.Description: Define bursty execution patterns of enumeration commands (e.g., <30s)
Tunable per org’s scripting/IT tools (e.g., exclude SCCM, PsExec)
Flag suspicious process ancestry (e.g., Word.exe spawning net.exe)

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Netsh Helper DLL Persistence via Registry and Child Process Monitoring (Windows)
Analysis Description: Detection focuses on monitoring registry modifications under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Netsh that indicate the addition of helper DLLs, followed by anomalous child process activity or module load behavior initiated by netsh.exe. These behaviors are rarely legitimate and may represent an adversary establishing persistence.

Mutable Elements.Description: Defines the time window in which correlated registry and execution events are considered suspicious (e.g., within 10 minutes)
List of expected or approved child processes spawned by netsh.exe in the enterprise environment
Directory or filename heuristics to distinguish benign DLLs from malicious helper DLLs

Detection Name: Email Forwarding Rule Abuse Detection Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Creation of inbox rules via PowerShell (New-InboxRule) or transport rules using Exchange cmdlets. Correlates user behavior, cmdlet usage, and rule properties.

Mutable Elements.Description: Certain service accounts or admin contexts may be expected to run these rules.
Correlate between rule creation and follow-on message forwarding within this timeframe.
Whitelisted or trusted destination addresses may be tuned per org policy.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Hijack Execution Flow through the KernelCallbackTable on Windows.
Analysis Description: Unexpected modification of the KernelCallbackTable in a process’s PEB followed by invocation of modified callback functions (e.g., fnCOPYDATA) through Windows messages. Defender observes suspicious API call chains such as NtQueryInformationProcess → WriteProcessMemory → abnormal GUI callback execution, often correlating to anomalous process behavior such as network activity or code injection.

Log Sources.Name: etw:Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Process
Log Sources.Channel: WriteProcessMemory: WriteProcessMemory targeting regions containing KernelCallbackTable addresses

Mutable Elements.Description: GUI applications (e.g., explorer.exe, notepad.exe) where KernelCallbackTable abuse is more likely.
Specific callback functions (e.g., fnCOPYDATA, fnDWORD) expected to remain stable.
Correlation interval between WriteProcessMemory calls and execution of modified callback functions.
Access rights values that should be flagged when targeting GUI processes.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Device Driver Discovery
Analysis Description: Monitor for suspicious usage of driver enumeration utilities (driverquery.exe) or API calls such as EnumDeviceDrivers(). Registry queries against HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services and HardwareProfiles that are abnormal may also indicate attempts to discover installed drivers and services. Correlate command execution, process creation, and registry access to build a behavioral chain of driver discovery.

Mutable Elements.Description: Whitelist expected administrative usage of driverquery.exe or other enumeration utilities.
Correlation window between process creation and registry queries to identify suspicious chaining of events.

Detection Name: Detect Network Provider DLL Registration and Credential Capture
Analysis Description: Detects registration of new or modified network provider DLLs via registry changes, anomalous file creation of DLLs in system directories, and suspicious process activity (mpnotify.exe interacting with non-standard DLLs). Multi-event correlation ties registry modification events to subsequent DLL loads during user logon activity.

Mutable Elements.Description: Specific registry keys to monitor for DLL registration (e.g., NetworkProvider Order).
Directories or file name patterns outside of normal system DLL locations.
Window correlating registry modification, DLL creation, and subsequent logon activity.

Detection Name: Detect One-Way Web Service Command Channels
Analysis Description: Suspicious process initiating outbound connections to web services without corresponding response or return traffic, indicative of one-way command channels.

Mutable Elements.Description: Can tune for popular web services (e.g., googleapis.com, github.com) based on threat actor tooling
May adjust temporal window to catch beaconing patterns (e.g., every 10-30 mins)
Environment-specific tuning to exclude expected update or telemetry tools

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for T1136 - Create Account across platforms
Analysis Description: Adversary uses built-in OS tools or API calls to create local or domain accounts for persistence or lateral movement. Tools such as 'net user', PowerShell, or MMC snap-ins may be used. Detection focuses on Event ID 4720 paired with process lineage and user context.

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlation between Event ID 4720 and creating process may vary by environment and automation delays
Tools like net.exe or powershell.exe can be normal or malicious depending on user context
System vs. administrator vs. low-privilege user context changes alert criticality

Detection Name: Behavior-chain detection strategy for T1127.003 Trusted Developer Utilities Proxy Execution: JamPlus (Windows)
Analysis Description: Abuse of JamPlus.exe to launch malicious payloads via crafted .jam files, resulting in abnormal process creation, command execution, or artifact generation outside of standard development workflows.

Log Sources.Name: WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-CodeIntegrity/Operational
Log Sources.Channel: Unsigned or untrusted modules loaded during JamPlus.exe runtime

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlation time window (e.g., 0–30 minutes) for JamPlus.exe execution, child processes, and file/network events.
Known developer systems where JamPlus.exe usage is expected; alerts are raised if executed elsewhere.
Child processes considered anomalous (e.g., PowerShell, cmd, wscript) when spawned by JamPlus.exe.
Regex patterns for non-standard or user-writable paths where JamPlus.exe drops artifacts.

Detection Name: Detection of NTDS.dit Credential Dumping from Domain Controllers
Analysis Description: Detects credential dumping attempts targeting the NTDS.dit database by monitoring shadow copy creation, suspicious file access to %SystemRoot%\NTDS\ntds.dit, and the use of tooling like ntdsutil.exe or volume management APIs.

Mutable Elements.Description: Tunable for NTDS file location or backup paths if organization uses custom domain controller storage structure.
Can suppress backup-related parent processes to reduce false positives.
Temporal correlation between shadow copy creation and NTDS file access (e.g., 5 min window).
Tune based on expected privileged user/service account behavior.

Detection Name: Enumeration of User or Account Information Across Platforms
Analysis Description: Detection of suspicious enumeration of local or domain accounts via command-line tools, WMI, or scripts.

Mutable Elements.Description: Match variations in enumeration commands like 'net user', 'Get-ADUser', 'dsquery'.
Short burst of account enumeration commands may indicate automation.
Restrict to non-admin accounts or unexpected users executing enumeration commands.

Detection Name: Detection fo Remote Service Session Hijacking for RDP.
Analysis Description: Detection of suspicious use of tscon.exe or equivalent methods to hijack legitimate RDP sessions. Defenders can observe anomalies such as session reassignments without corresponding authentication, processes spawned in the context of hijacked sessions, or unusual RDP network traffic flows that deviate from expected baselines.

Mutable Elements.Description: Whitelist of systems and accounts authorized to use RDP; deviations indicate possible hijacking.
Time threshold for correlating logon events with session reassignment and process execution.
Environment-specific mapping of user accounts to session IDs; inconsistencies may reveal hijacking.

Detection Name: Detect Modification of Authentication Process via Reversible Encryption
Analysis Description: Detects enabling of reversible password encryption in Active Directory or Group Policy, suspicious PowerShell commands modifying AD user properties, and unusual account configuration changes correlated with policy modifications. Multi-event correlation links Group Policy edits, PowerShell command execution, and user account property changes to identify tampering with authentication encryption settings.

Mutable Elements.Description: Scope of Organizational Units where reversible encryption property monitoring is enabled.
Time window in which to correlate Group Policy modification and subsequent user property changes.
List of PowerShell cmdlets to monitor for account configuration changes.

Detection Name: Behavioral Detection of External Website Defacement across Platforms
Analysis Description: Adversary modifies externally-facing web content by accessing and overwriting hosted HTML/JS/CSS files, typically following web shell deployment, credential abuse, or exploitation of web application vulnerabilities.

Log Sources.Name: NSM:Connections
Log Sources.Channel: Unusual POST requests to admin or upload endpoints

Mutable Elements.Description: Web root folder varies by environment, e.g., C:\inetpub\wwwroot
May vary based on which service account hosts the website
Time between webshell upload and file overwrite may vary

Detection Name: Cross-Platform Behavioral Detection of File Timestomping via Metadata Tampering
Analysis Description: Detects attempts to modify file timestamps via API usage (e.g., SetFileTime), CLI tools (e.g., w32tm, PowerShell), or double-timestomp behavior where $SI and $FN timestamps are mismatched or reverted.

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlate timestamp change with preceding file creation or suspicious access
Include SetFileTime, NtSetInformationFile, or other timestamp APIs
Trigger on excessive backdating (e.g., >90 days)

Detection Name: Detection of Unauthorized DCSync Operations via Replication API Abuse
Analysis Description: Detects unauthorized invocation of replication operations (DCSync) via Directory Replication Service (DRS), often executed by threat actors using Mimikatz or similar tools from non-DC endpoints.

Mutable Elements.Description: Defines the correlation window for unusual account access followed by DRSUAPI traffic.
Allows tuning for specific accounts known to legitimately request replication.
Expected replication should only come from known DCs; this field allows excluding trusted DCs.

Detection Name: Detection Strategy for Exploitation for Defense Evasion
Analysis Description: Detects exploitation attempts targeting defensive security software or OS services. Defender observation includes abnormal process behavior (e.g., AV or EDR crashing unexpectedly), unsigned/untrusted modules loaded into defensive processes, or privilege escalation from security agent services. Multi-event correlation ties exploitation attempts to subsequent evasive behavior like service termination or missing logs.

Mutable Elements.Description: List of defensive services/processes (e.g., AV, EDR) monitored in the environment.
Whitelisted DLL/module paths normally loaded by defensive tools.
Number of abnormal terminations of defensive processes tolerated before triggering an alert.

Detection Name: Detect Unauthorized Access to Password Managers
Analysis Description: Detection of suspicious access to password manager processes (KeePass, 1Password, LastPass, Bitwarden) through abnormal process injection, memory reads, or command-line usage of vault-related DLLs. Correlates process creation with OS API calls and file access to vault databases (.kdbx, .opvault, .ldb).

Mutable Elements.Description: List of monitored binaries and file formats for password managers in use (e.g., KeePass, 1Password, Bitwarden, LastPass).
Window to correlate process creation, API access, and file reads indicative of credential extraction.
Filter for administrative accounts vs. expected users of password managers.

Detection Name: Detection of Selective Exclusion
Analysis Description: A process with no prior history or outside of known whitelisted tools initiates file or registry modifications to configure exclusion rules for antivirus, backup, or file-handling systems. Or a file system enumeration for specific file names andcritical extensions like .dll, .exe, .sys, or specific directories such as 'Program Files' or security tool paths or system component discovery for the exclusion of the files or components.

Mutable Elements.Description: Correlate multiply discovery activities and file enumeration activities.
Minimum number of different discovery techniques within time window to trigger detection - balance between false positives and coverage (default: 4 activities)
List of extensions or folders considered suspicious when excluded (e.g., .dll, .exe, C:\\Program Files\\)
Whitelist of known system management tools/processes allowed to modify exclusion settings

Detection Name: Detection of Spoofed User-Agent
Analysis Description: Process execution without GUI context (e.g., powershell.exe, wscript.exe) generates HTTP traffic with a spoofed User-Agent mimicking a legitimate browser. No corresponding UI application (e.g., msedge.exe) is active or in parent lineage. The User-Agent deviates from known enterprise baselines or contains spoofed platform indicators.  User-Agent strings can be gathered with API calls such as ShellExecuteW to open the default browser on a socket to receive an HTTP reply, or by hard coding the User-Agent string for a specific browser.

Log Sources.Name: NSM:Flow
Log Sources.Channel: Inbound HTTP POST with suspicious payload size or user-agent

Mutable Elements.Description: Specific HTTP header anomalies or patterns (e.g., spoofed User-Agent).
Flag browser-based sessions
List of non-browser binaries expected not to initiate web requests (e.g., powershell.exe, cscript.exe)
